title: Shrinking AI memory boosts accuracy, study finds
abstract: Inference-time scaling trades efficiency for increased reasoning accuracy by gener- ating longer or more parallel sequences. However, in Transformer LLMs, genera- tion cost is bottlenecked by the size of the key–value (KV) cache, rather than the number of generated tokens. Hence, we explore inference-time hyper-scaling: by compressing the KV cache, we can generate more tokens within the same compute budget and further improve the accuracy of scaled inference. The success of this approach, however, hinges on the ability of compression methods to preserve accuracy even at high compression ratios. To make hyper-scaling practical, we introduce Dynamic Memory Sparsification (DMS), a novel method for sparsifying KV caches that only requires 1K training steps to achieve 8× compression, while maintaining better accuracy than training-free sparse attention. Instead of prema- turely discarding cached tokens, DMS delays token eviction, implicitly merging representations and preserving critical information. We demonstrate the effective- ness of inference-time hyper-scaling with DMS on multiple families of LLMs, showing that it boosts accuracy for comparable inference latency and memory load. For instance, we enhance Qwen-R1 32B by 12.0 points on AIME 24, 8.6 on GPQA, and 9.7 on LiveCodeBench on average for an equivalent number of memory reads.
introduction:
Introduction

Scaling inference-time compute—employed in mod- els such as OpenAI’s o1 (OpenAI et al., 2024) or DeepSeek’s R1 (Guo et al., 2025)—trades off in- creased inference time and memory for higher rea- soning accuracy in large language models (LLMs). Models reason by generating intermediate steps that explore the problem before reaching an answer. Ad- justing the depth and breadth of this exploration— known as sequential and parallel scaling, respectively (Muennighoff et al., 2025)—controls the inference- time compute budget (Yao et al., 2023; Uesato et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023; Lightman et al., 2024).

LiveCodeBench

AIME24

MATH 500

R1-Qwen 2.5 7B

GPQA

Despite its success, scaling inference-time compute is fundamentally bottlenecked in Transformer LLMs by the number of tokens from the key–value (or KV) cache that are attended to during auto-regressive gen- eration. This cache grows linearly with respect to the length and number of reasoning chains, as the new key–value representations are appended to it.

Figure 1: Average absolute gains of DMS over the original LLMs during inference-time scaling on reasoning tasks for the same KV cache memory reads, (a proxy for latency).

∗Work done as an intern at NVIDIA.

Hence, it can easily exhaust the memory of the accelerator and slow down each generation step, as attention is memory-bound: its cost is dominated by the time needed to retrieve the cache from memory. Fortunately, several methods can mitigate these issues during post-training or inference. These rely on training-free heuristics to sparsify the KV cache (Oren et al., 2024; Li et al., 2024), selectively retrieve subsets of tokens (Tang et al., 2024), or retrofit LLMs with the ability to choose whether to merge or append items to the cache (Nawrot et al., 2024).

In this work, we investigate for the first time whether efficient attention methods enhance inference- time scaling. In principle, by exploring more concurrent reasoning threads or longer threads for the same memory or runtime budget, efficient models can achieve higher-quality predictions than their original counterparts. However, this hinges upon the crucial assumption that efficient attention does not degrade the model’s reasoning abilities, which unfortunately is often the side effect of training-free sparsification methods (Zhang et al., 2023a; Oren et al., 2024). On the other hand, KV cache compression during post-training usually better preserves the model’s quality, but also demands costly retrofitting procedures (Nawrot et al., 2024).

In order to overcome these limitations, as a second main contribution, we propose Dynamic Memory Sparsification (DMS), a new method that combines the best of both worlds by retrofitting LLMs to sparsify the KV cache through an inexpensive procedure. We thus demonstrate that sparsification— rather than more complex token merging proposed in Dynamic Memory Compression (DMC; Nawrot et al., 2024)—is sufficient to retain accuracy at high compression ratios. This, in turn, allows us to retrofit LLMs with KV cache compression in a much more sample-efficient way than DMC, achieving 8× compression with only 1K training steps. On the other hand, the superior performance of DMS highlights the benefits of retrofitting LLMs over training-free heuristics.

We evaluate inference-time scaling capabilities of efficient attention (including DMS) on reasoning datasets such as MATH-500 (Hendrycks et al., 2021b) and AIME 2024 for math, GPQA Diamond (Rein et al., 2024) for hard sciences, and LiveCodeBench (Jain et al., 2025) for coding. We find that DMS significantly improves the Pareto frontiers across various model sizes and datasets, outper- forming vanilla LLMs in both memory reads (which is a proxy for runtime) and peak memory use. Notably, DMS consistently dominates other baselines for efficient attention, which we also verify on a broader set of tasks outside of inference-time scaling. DMS variants even surpass the corresponding vanilla LLMs on long-context tasks, such as needle-in-a-haystack and variable state tracking (Hsieh et al., 2024), while achieving higher efficiency. Overall, this validates the effectiveness of efficient attention—unlocked by DMS—for inference-time scaling, which improves the reasoning capabilities of models under any given inference-time budget.

Headline:  
MIT’s Attention Matching Signals New Era for LLM Inference Efficiency—Dynamic Memory Sparsification Emerges as Key Complement in KV Cache Compression

News Article:  
On March 6, 2026, VentureBeat reported on a significant advance from MIT researchers: a Key-Value (KV) cache compaction technique, “Attention Matching,” that slashes large language model (LLM) memory overhead by a factor of 50 without sacrificing accuracy. This breakthrough arrives as enterprises face strict memory ceilings and prohibitive hardware costs when deploying LLMs on massive documents and long-horizon tasks. The MIT team’s technique highlights a critical challenge in LLM engineering—efficiently compressing the working memory during inference to prevent out-of-memory (OOM) errors and accelerate real-world adoption.

This challenge of efficient KV cache usage is not isolated; it is a founding constraint for both academic and commercial innovation within transformer-based LLMs. The KV cache functions as the model’s “working memory,” storing representations from previous context tokens. Because the size of this cache grows linearly with the length and number of concurrent reasoning chains, LLM inference can quickly saturate accelerator memory, driving up latency and hardware demands. The problem—central to both MIT’s “Attention Matching” and the latest research on Dynamic Memory Sparsification (DMS)—is thus one of bottlenecked memory and compute efficiency at inference time.

Transformer LLMs, such as OpenAI’s o1 and DeepSeek’s R1, have demonstrated that greater inference-time compute—by expanding reasoning steps or parallelism—improves model accuracy. However, according to recent findings ([Citation]: https://openreview.net/pdf?id=8ZiElzQxf1), further gains are blocked not by core model size, but by the number of tokens retained in the KV cache. The cache’s linear growth fundamentally limits both the achievable context window and the practical deployment footprint, especially under enterprise resource constraints. This memory-induced bottleneck has propelled novel solutions—including MIT’s method and DMS—that compress the KV cache while maintaining, or improving, accuracy.

Dynamic Memory Sparsification (DMS) introduces a paradigm shift in KV cache compression during LLM inference-time scaling. Unlike training-free heuristic sparsification methods, which typically degrade reasoning ability at higher compression ratios, DMS leverages a brief retrofitting process—reportedly achieving 8× compression with only 1,000 training steps—without accuracy compromise over baseline models. DMS accomplishes this by delaying token eviction and implicitly merging context representations, preserving critical historical information. This stands in contrast to methods requiring costly retrofitting for complex token merging (such as Dynamic Memory Compression), demonstrating that principled sparsification suffices to retain performance at high compression.

Empirical evaluation across benchmarks—AIME 2024, MATH-500, GPQA Diamond, and LiveCodeBench—reveals that DMS boosts accuracy for equivalent numbers of memory reads, consistently surpassing both vanilla LLM performance and alternative sparsification baselines. For example, on the Qwen-R1 32B model, DMS delivers mean accuracy gains of 12.0 points on AIME 24, 8.6 on GPQA, and 9.7 on LiveCodeBench, under fixed KV cache memory budgets. This positions DMS at the Pareto frontier of efficiency and accuracy, extending LLM capabilities for long-context reasoning and variable state tracking within feasible hardware constraints.

The practical and theoretical implications of these advances reverberate throughout the AI infrastructure landscape. As Andrej Karpathy emphasizes in his “March of Nines” thesis, the road to reliability in large-scale AI systems is not linear: while reaching 90% reliability is tractable, each successive improvement—toward 99% or 99.9%—demands disproportionately more engineering rigor. Here, memory-efficient inference techniques such as DMS and MIT’s “Attention Matching” provide the necessary substrate to attain the reliability, scalability, and stability expected in enterprise deployments, by systematically alleviating memory bottlenecks that provoke model degradation or OOM failures during complex, long-context tasks.

These innovations cohere around a single technical axis: inference-time KV cache management as the linchpin of transformer scalability. The DMS research empirically demonstrates that focused, efficient attention mechanisms can be retrofitted into existing LLMs with minimal training cost, without incurring the quality deficits associated with training-free heuristics. The MIT team’s work, achieving higher compression ratios, further substantiates the strategic significance of KV cache optimization as a precondition for sustained model accuracy under enterprise-level memory and latency constraints.

Technical stakeholders, model developers, and AI researchers are thus advised to engage directly with primary research in this area. DMS sets a new benchmark for memory-efficient inference-time scaling and, in coordination with advanced compaction methods like MIT’s Attention Matching, charts a clear path toward robust, production-grade, and cost-effective LLM services.

For comprehensive analysis, experimental details, and implementation specifics, consult the full DMS research paper at [https://openreview.net/pdf?id=8ZiElzQxf1](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=8ZiElzQxf1).

For enterprises struggling with the high hardware costs of AI, this represents a major paradigm shift.

In Transformer models, the KV cache functions as the model's 'working memory,' storing context from previous tokens to ensure coherent generation. However, as the context window grows, the KV cache expands linearly, often leading to OOM (Out-of-Memory) errors on standard GPUs. The MIT team’s breakthrough leverages dynamic matching of attention patterns, stripping away redundant historical data without affecting the model's semantic understanding. This work builds upon the foundations laid by researchers in papers like _FlashAttention-4_ and _POET-X_, but offers a significantly higher compression ratio for real-time inference.

Complementing this technical leap is a strategic warning from AI luminary Andrej Karpathy. He has framed the current state of industry development as the 'March of Nines.' Karpathy argues that while achieving 90% reliability is easy for a demo, adding each additional '9'—moving to 99%, then 99.9%—requires exponential engineering effort. Reliability is the primary barrier to enterprise adoption, and memory-efficient techniques like the one from MIT are crucial in preventing model degradation during long-form analysis, moving the industry closer to that elusive production-grade stability.

title: Creating realistic 3D scenes from everyday online photos
abstract:
Figure 1: WildCAT3D. (top) We use large image collections captured in the wild to train our feed-forward novel-view synthesis model. (middle) At inference time, WildCAT3D can generate full scene-level novel views from a single image of a new (never-before encountered) scene. (bottom) It can also be used to control the appearance of the generated views, e.g. via a text prompt.

Despite recent advances in sparse novel view synthesis (NVS) applied to object- centric scenes, scene-level NVS remains a challenge. A central issue is the lack of available clean multi-view training data, beyond manually curated datasets with limited diversity, camera variation, or licensing issues. On the other hand, an abundance of diverse and permissively-licensed data exists in the wild, consisting of scenes with varying appearances (illuminations, transient occlusions, etc.) from sources such as tourist photos. To this end, we present WildCAT3D, a framework for generating novel views of scenes learned from diverse 2D scene image data cap- tured in the wild. We unlock training on these data sources by explicitly modeling global appearance conditions in images, extending the state-of-the-art multi-view diffusion paradigm to learn from scene views of varying appearances. Our trained model generalizes to new scenes at inference time, enabling the generation of multiple consistent novel views. WildCAT3D provides state-of-the-art results on single-view NVS in object- and scene-level settings, while training on strictly fewer data sources than prior methods. Additionally, it enables novel applications by providing global appearance control during generation.
introduction:
Introduction

Imagine observing the Golden Gate Bridge from afar at different viewpoints as the weather conditions change – in fog, bright sunlight, in rain and at night. From these observations, one can intuit the 3D structure of the Golden Gate Bridge, and can likely imagine how similar bridges might appear from various viewpoints. Our work applies a similar intuition to novel view synthesis (NVS) – the task of predicting a new 2D view of a scene which has been partially observed – to generate views of new scenes by learning from observations differing in global appearance. While recent progress in NVS has been achieved by leveraging powerful multi-view diffusion models, as popularized by CAT3D Gao et al. [2024], these models typically have poor applicability to full scenes due to scarce clean multi-view data. As such, these models typically build off pretrained image generation models which are fine-tuned on limited datasets of synthetic renderings or crowd-sourced videos capturing isolated objects, with poor applicability to full scenes like the MegaScenes collection [Tung et al., 2024]. On the other hand, such scenes are abundantly covered in permissively-licensed image collections captured in the wild from the Internet, in which scene views differ greatly in appearance (e.g., aspect ratios, lighting, weather conditions or transient occlusions), making them incompatible with existing multi-view diffusion architectures. In this work, we unlock their ability to learn from this scalable source of readily-available, diverse, and permissively licensed scene data.

We propose WildCAT3D, a multi-view diffusion model à la CAT3D which can be learned from in-the-wild Internet data through appearance awareness. Our key insight is that inconsistent data can be leveraged during multi-view diffusion training to learn consistent generation, by specifically decoupling content and appearance when denoising novel views. More concretely, starting from the standard multi-view diffusion framework of CAT3D, we propose to explicitly integrate a feed- forward and generalizable appearance model whose goal is to capture the appearance properties of the input views. We do so by adding an appearance encoding branch to the model, designed to produce low-dimensional appearance embeddings that are used as an extra conditioning signal for the multi-view diffusion model. This branch is trained simultaneously with the diffusion model and a custom classifier-free guidance mechanism is applied to avoid oversaturation artifacts. During inference, the appearance embedding from the source view is injected to the target views, which allows us to preserve the appearance across the generated views. Intuitively, these design choices allow our model to “peek” at coarse appearance signals such as weather condition and aspect ratio, without leaking too much information about the target views to denoise.

In addition, in order to improve the viewpoint consistency, we augment our appearance-aware model by adapting a warp conditioning mechanism to the context of our multi-view diffusion framework. More specifically, for each target view to denoise, pixels from the source view are warped following the target viewpoint using a known depth map, and the resulting image is injected as an additional conditioning signal into the diffusion model. Intuitively, such a mechanism approximately indicates the correct placement of the scene, thus resolving the scale ambiguity that is inherent to the single-view NVS problem.

We exhaustively compare our method on standard NVS benchmarks and demonstrate superior Importantly, this performance, while training on fewer curated data sources than prior works. highlights the strength of our work in leveraging a larger set of samples from existing, permissively licensed imagery captured in-the-wild, rather than relying on heavily curated datasets. Our results also show strong performance on diverse scenes captured in tourist photos, including static video generation from single input frames and custom camera trajectories. In addition, our explicit modeling of appearance both allows us to learn from in-the-wild data to successfully generate consistent views of full scenes and also enables novel applications such as interpolation between views of differing appearances or NVS with appearance control via text, as showcased in Figure 1.

In summary, our key contributions are three-fold:

A new appearance-aware multi-view diffusion model able to learn from in-the-wild images, • Performance superior to state-of-the-art methods on single-view NVS benchmarks, • Novel applications enabled by NVS with controlled appearances.

Headline:  
WildCAT3D, WorldGen, and GWM-1 Mark Structural Shift from Flat Images to Automated 3D Scene Generation

News Article:  
A new era in spatial AI is emerging as recent models such as WildCAT3D, Meta’s WorldGen, and Runway’s GWM-1 demonstrate a fundamental transition: generating fully navigable, editable 3D environments directly from single images or text prompts. Meta’s WorldGen, for instance, exemplifies this leap by transforming weeks of manual 3D asset development into five minutes of AI-driven scene synthesis, producing interactive 3D worlds with navigation meshes and game engine–ready assets from simple language cues. Similarly, WildCAT3D sets a new benchmark by generating realistic scene-level novel views from single images collected “in the wild,” while Runway’s GWM-1 establishes real-time, consistent world generation and interactive video via controllable parameters. Collectively, these systems do not merely represent an upgrade in visual fidelity, but evidence a structural shift from static pixel arrays toward dynamic, spatially-coherent digital environments with explicit geometry, physics, and interactive semantics.

At the core of WildCAT3D’s technical achievement is its explicit modeling of global appearance conditions—a method that resolves the traditional impediment of data scarcity for scene-level novel view synthesis (NVS). Prevailing approaches in multi-view diffusion have required carefully curated multi-view data, typically constraining applicability to object-centric scenes due to limited dataset diversity and strict licensing. In contrast, WildCAT3D unlocks scalable training from diverse, permissively-licensed Internet image collections where appearances vary widely, encompassing differences in aspect ratio, lighting, weather, and transient occlusions (Abstract; Introduction). The key innovation is the decoupling of scene content from appearance: during training, WildCAT3D integrates a feed-forward appearance model to yield low-dimensional appearance embeddings as additional conditioning for its multi-view diffusion network. By injecting these embeddings during inference, the system preserves global appearance consistency across generated views, while enabling control via appearance-aware signals—a capability also extended to text-prompted appearance manipulation.

This architectural strategy addresses the ill-posedness and ambiguity inherent to single-view scene synthesis. Beyond appearance modeling, WildCAT3D augments viewpoint consistency by introducing a warp conditioning mechanism: for each denoised target view, pixels from the input image are warped to the target viewpoint using a known depth map and supplied as further conditioning. This design leverages the structural cues necessary for disambiguating scale and spatial layout, facilitating coherent synthesis even from sparse observations (Introduction).

The practical implications are underscored by exhaustive evaluations on NVS benchmarks: WildCAT3D demonstrates superior performance in single-view NVS for both object- and scene-level scenarios, all while strictly utilizing fewer curated data resources than prior methods (Abstract; Introduction). Notably, the model demonstrates robust generalization to new scenes at inference, supporting applications such as static video creation from a single input image, custom camera trajectory rendering, and appearance interpolation driven by user prompts. These features point directly to the scalability and real-world applicability of WildCAT3D, echoing the rapid, automated, and physically consistent scene generation demonstrated by Meta’s WorldGen (Real-World Anchor Event).

Meta’s WorldGen, as reported, lowers the barrier for 3D content generation by eliminating specialized hardware requirements and using a layered, agentic system to parse text prompts, reconstruct logical layouts, and output navigation meshes. The system’s ability to generate environments that support interactive physics and valid navigation mirrors WildCAT3D’s focus on generating not merely visually plausible but spatially consistent scenes—rooting both approaches in explicit handling of structural realism. Meanwhile, Runway’s GWM-1 advances “world models” for controllable, real-time video simulation and synthetic robotics data generation, reinforcing the necessity for models that disentangle content from appearance to derive physical consistency across generated scenes.

Thus, the architectural decoupling in WildCAT3D—appearance-aware conditioning and warp-based geometry induction—directly parallels the technical advances propelling spatial AI toward automation, consistency, and editability in 3D world synthesis. Each system leverages scalable, diverse data and modular architectures to bridge the historical chasm between fixed media and dynamic, physically-grounded virtual spaces.

For researchers seeking to investigate the full technical details, design decisions, and empirical benchmarks that drive this transformation, the complete WildCAT3D research paper is accessible at [https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.13030](https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.13030). Direct engagement with these contributions is essential for those advancing the state of the art in spatial AI, world models, and data-driven 3D scene understanding.

Meta's WorldGen collapses weeks of 3D production into five minutes, showing off what AI automation can do for spatial computing. WorldGen is a generative AI system developed by Meta's Reality Labs that constructs fully interactive 3D environments from single text prompts. The system produces game engine ready assets capable of supporting physics, navigation, and collision detection, not just static visual renderings. The architecture employs a modular four stage pipeline mirroring traditional development workflows. An LLM first performs scene planning by parsing the text input and generating a logical layout, while subsequent stages handle reconstruction, decomposition into editable objects, and high-resolution enhancement. The system generates navigation meshes alongside visual geometry, ensuring that a "ancient East Asian temple" prompt produces not just visually coherent structures but spatially accessible environments where streets remain unobstructed and physics remain valid. WorldGen outputs standard textured meshes compatible with industry platforms like Unity and Unreal Engine enabling direct integration into existing production pipelines. This approach eliminates the specialized rendering hardware typically required by competing methods, lowering friction for creation and adoption. While a great leap forward, current limitations hinder its scope. Generation relies on a single reference point, restricting scalability to scenes spanning roughly 50×50 meters. This does show off how layered multi-agent (agentic) systems can break down and handle complex tasks to generate results traditional LLMs cannot do on their own. Runway has just announced GWM-1, their first general world model family that represents a significant leap forward in AI's ability to simulate reality in real time. This state-of-the-art model builds on their Gen-4.5 foundation and generates video frame by frame with interactive control through camera poses, robot commands, and audio inputs. GWM-1 comes in three specialized variants, each addressing critical industry needs. GWM Worlds creates infinite explorable environments with consistent geometry, lighting, and physics, enabling players to move freely through coherent spaces without manual design. GWM Avatars delivers audio-driven interactive video generation with realistic facial expressions, lip-syncing, and gestures for extended conversations, perfect for education, customer service, and training simulations. GWM Robotics serves as a learned simulator that generates synthetic data for scalable robot training and policy evaluation, removing physical hardware bottlenecks. What makes this particularly compelling is the vision behind it. Runway believes that language models alone won't solve challenges in robotics, disease research, or scientific discovery. Real progress requires models that experience the world and learn from mistakes, just as humans do. World models accelerate this trial-and-error learning by enabling it to happen in simulation rather than the real world, offering the clearest path to general-purpose simulation. The platform also introduces powerful updates to Gen-4.5, including native audio generation and editing capabilities, plus multi-shot video editing that allows consistent transformations across videos of arbitrary length. The model can generate up to 2 minutes of 720p video and supports customization for specific actions and domains. This announcement signals a fundamental shift in how we think about AI systems interacting with and simulating reality. The implications for gaming, education, robotics, VR experiences, and agent training are enormous. title: Generative AIs fail at the game of visual telephone
abstract: Autonomous AI-to-AI creative systems promise new frontiers in machine creativity, yet we show that they systematically converge toward generic outputs. We built iterative feedback loops between Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL; image generation) and Large Language and Vision Assistant (LLaVA; image description), forming autonomous text → image → text → image cycles. Across 700 trajectories with diverse prompts and 7 temperature settings over 100 iterations, all runs converged to nearly identical visuals—what we term “visual elevator music.” Quantitative analysis revealed just 12 dominant motifs with commercially safe aesthetics, such as stormy lighthouses and palatial interiors. This convergence persisted across model pairs, indicating structural limits in cross-modal AI creativity. The effect mirrors human cultural transmission, where iterated learning amplifies cognitive biases, but here, diversity collapses entirely as AI loops gravitate to high-probability attractors in training data. Our findings expose hidden homogenizing tendencies in current architectures and underscore the need for anti-convergence mechanisms and sustained human-AI interplay to preserve creative diversity.
introduction: Artificial intelligence (AI) systems exhibit systematic behavioral changes when operating in iterative workflows, a phenomenon that has emerged as a fundamental challenge across multiple domains. In human-in-the-loop systems, researchers have documented consistent drift patterns where AI recommendations gradually shift user preferences toward mainstream, predictable choices.1,2 During model training, the phenomenon of model collapse demonstrates that AI systems trained on recursively generated data inevitably lose diversity, with the tails of content distributions disappearing as models converge toward high-probability, generic outputs.3,4 From a dynamical systems perspective, these convergence patterns reflect fundamental properties of optimization landscapes, where iterative processes naturally evolve toward stable attractors.5,6. AI deployment now increasingly relies on agentic architectures. In these systems, AI evaluates, critiques, and iterates on its own outputs. Large language models (LLMs) now routinely judge the quality of generated text, critique reasoning steps, and refine responses through self-reflection.7 Visual AI systems increasingly operate in workflows where one model generates content and another assesses its quality, alignment, or aesthetic merit.8 These agentic approaches represent a shift from human-supervised to machine-supervised AI workflows, where algorithms make autonomous judgments about their own creative and analytical outputs. This trend extends naturally to fully AI-to-AI creative systems, where multiple models collaborate without human intervention to generate, evaluate, and iterate on creative content.10,11 Such systems promise significant advantages: they can operate continuously, scale beyond human oversight constraints, and potentially explore creative territories unconstrained by human aesthetic biases.12,13 In principle, these autonomous creative loops should maintain focus and “stay on point”—if an AI system describes an image and another regenerates that image, the content should remain semantically stable, perhaps even improving through iterative refinement. This raises a fundamental question: do autonomous AI-to-AI creative systems maintain their intended focus, or do they drift toward generic outputs? Unlike training-time collapse or human-mediated drift, this represents a novel regime where inference-time dynamics in frozen, pre-trained models determine system behavior.14,15 The cross-modal nature of many creative workflows—where semantic representations must remain consistent across different neural architectures—creates additional constraints absent in single-modal systems. This investigation examines the stability of autonomous AI-to-AI creative loops through systematic experimentation with state-of-the-art generative models. While creativity researchers have theoretically predicted that AI systems might produce formulaic outputs due to their lack of genuine agency and intentionality,17,18,19 empirical validation of these predictions—and discovery of their specific manifestations—remains essential. Our work provides this empirical grounding, not only revealing that convergence occurs but characterizing its speed, robustness, and the particular visual attractors that emerge. We constructed iterative feedback loops between Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL) for image generation and Large Language and Vision Assistant (LLaVA) for image description, creating closed text-image-text-image cycles that operate without human intervention.20,21 To ensure robust analysis, we initiated hundreds of independent trajectories from semantically diverse starting points. We used reservoir-based novelty search22 and tested system behavior across multiple temperature conditions over extended iteration periods. The results reveal a striking and counterintuitive phenomenon: despite the stochastic nature of both image generation and text description, autonomous AI-to-AI creative loops consistently converge toward remarkably similar outputs. Independent trajectories, regardless of their diverse semantic starting points or sampling parameters, evolve toward nearly identical visual and textual endpoints characterized by generic, commercially viable aesthetics—what we term “visual elevator music.” Quantitative analysis reveals convergence toward just 12 dominant visual motifs across all experimental conditions, suggesting fundamental constraints in cross-modal AI-to-AI creative processes that transcend individual model architectures. Our findings challenge assumptions about the open-ended nature of autonomous AI systems and reveal previously unknown limitations in machine creativity.23,24 Rather than maintaining creative focus or exploring diverse possibilities, AI-to-AI loops exhibit systematic drift toward high-probability attractors embedded within their training distributions.25,26,27 The robustness of this convergence across temperature conditions indicates that the phenomenon reflects deep architectural constraints rather than sampling artifacts, with significant implications for AI-assisted creativity and the deployment of autonomous creative systems.28,29,30

Headline:  
AI Model Collapse: Empirical Evidence of Convergence in Autonomous Creative Loops Exposes Limits of Machine-Driven Innovation

News Article:

Technologists have grown increasingly apprehensive about “model collapse”—a process whereby AI-generated content recursively feeds back into AI systems, producing self-reinforcing homogenization and a collapse of conceptual and stylistic diversity. As detailed in The Atlantic’s coverage, this phenomenon is not merely a hypothetical risk. Rather, it has profound implications for the future capabilities of generative AI systems, as recursive creative cycles amplify the tendency of neural networks to converge on generic, high-probability outputs at the expense of innovation and novelty (source: The Atlantic, 2025).

A recent study published in *Patterns* provides rigorous empirical validation for these concerns through the lens of autonomous AI-to-AI creative systems. The research interrogates whether closed-loop workflows—wherein state-of-the-art generative models iterate without human supervision—preserve, refine, or instead erode diversity in creative output. By structuring iterative feedback cycles between Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL), an image generator, and Large Language and Vision Assistant (LLaVA), an image captioner, the investigation systematically probes the mechanism of convergence alluded to by model collapse discourse in both the media and expert communities ([Citation](https://www.cell.com/patterns/fulltext/S2666-3899(25)00299-5)).

Crucially, the experimental design entailed initiating 700 independent trajectories, each seeded with semantically diverse prompts and subject to a variety of sampling temperature settings. Over 100 full cycles—each spanning a text→image→text→image sequence—researchers observed that, regardless of initial conditions or variance parameters, all runs converged to a narrow set of nearly identical visuals. This phenomenon, colloquially termed “visual elevator music,” is quantitatively evidenced by the emergence of just 12 dominant motifs, characterized by commercially safe and high-probability content such as stormy lighthouses and palatial interiors (Abstract; Introduction).

This robust convergence was found not to be an artifact of sampling or specific to a single architecture. Rather, the effect persisted across model pairs and parameter settings, exposing structural limitations inherent in contemporary cross-modal generative loops. The results connect directly to concerns articulated in model collapse discourse: when AI systems iteratively critique and refine one another’s output without human intervention, diversity does not merely drift but collapses entirely—leading to an ecosystem dominated by what the authors identify as high-probability attractors embedded within the original training distributions.

From a technical standpoint, this investigation reveals that convergence in autonomous AI-to-AI creative loops is not equivalent to collapse arising during model training with synthetic data, nor does it map precisely to “human-in-the-loop” drift. Instead, this collapse represents a novel regime, wherein inference-time dynamics—distinct from training-time collapse—become the principal determinants of system behavior, even as models remain frozen and ostensibly “creativity-focused” workflows are preserved (Introduction). In other words, the recursive use of self-generated material acts as a structural amplifier of prior biases and limitations, further narrowing the expressivity of even the most sophisticated pre-trained generative models, absent sustained human intervention.

From a dynamical systems perspective, the study situates these findings within the broader context of optimization landscapes and attractor dynamics, echoing the established theoretical prediction that iterated learning—in both human culture and artificial systems—leans towards stable, mainstream solutions at the expense of tail diversity. However, the empirical confirmation that AI-to-AI loops amplify this effect to the point of near-total collapse represents a critical inflection point for the deployment of agentic architectures ([Introduction](https://www.cell.com/patterns/fulltext/S2666-3899(25)00299-5)). The robustness of convergence, irrespective of temperature settings or initial semantic diversity, indicates that this is not a simple failure of parameter tuning, but a deep architectural constraint potentially endemic to current cross-modal, multimodal generative models.

The implications for AI-assisted creativity—and for those deploying autonomous creative systems at scale—are substantial. These findings challenge the prevailing assumption that architecting AI systems to self-critique or autonomously co-create will inherently preserve, or even foster, creativity and diversity. The research underscores the urgency of developing explicit anti-convergence strategies and reintroducing sustained human-AI interplay to maintain diversity, as absent such intervention, the underlying architectures risk institutionalizing the very homogeneity that technologists warn may threaten the generative potential of AI ([Abstract], Introduction).

For those seeking to critically engage with the technical details and explore the quantitative dynamics underlying this convergence phenomenon, the authors’ full study is accessible and merits careful review by the research community. 

Read the full research article at [Patterns](https://www.cell.com/patterns/fulltext/S2666-3899(25)00299-5) to examine the empirical methodology, underlying mechanisms, and proposed mitigations in detail.

title: Flexible position encoding helps LLMs follow complex instructions and shifting states
abstract: The attention mechanism is a core primitive in modern large language models (LLMs) and AI more broadly. Since attention by itself is permutation-invariant, position encoding is essential for modeling structured domains such as language. Rotary position encoding (RoPE) has emerged as the de facto standard approach for position encoding and is part of many modern LLMs. However, in RoPE the key/query transformation between two elements in a sequence is only a function of their relative position and otherwise independent of the actual input. This limits the expressivity of RoPE-based transformers. This paper describes PaTH, a flexible data-dependent position encoding scheme based on accumulated products of Householder(like) transformations, where each transformation is data-dependent, i.e., a function of the input. We derive an efficient parallel algorithm for training through exploiting a compact representation of products of Householder matrices, and implement a FlashAttention-style blockwise algorithm that minimizes I/O cost. Across both targeted synthetic benchmarks and moderate-scale real-world language modeling experiments, we find that PaTH demonstrates superior performance compared to RoPE and other recent baselines.
introduction:
Introduction

Attention mechanisms form the backbone of transformer architectures that power contemporary AI systems. Attention is inherently permutation-invariant, and thus encoding positional information into attention is important for effective sequence modeling. Since the original sinusoidal embeddings [74], various position encoding schemes have been proposed over the years [16, 59, 25, 22, 42, 55, 69, inter alia]; see Dufter et al. [17] for a comprehensive survey. Among these, rotary position embedding [RoPE; 69] has emerged as the de facto standard, adopted in most recent state-of-the-art LLMs.

RoPE works by transforming the key (kj) and query (qi) embeddings through a rotation matrix R whose rotation angle is a function of the difference in positions, resulting in the bilinear form q⊤ i Ri−jkj for the attention logits. The rotation matrix R itself is a block-diagonal matrix composed of two-by-two rotation matrices, which enables efficient computation. However, the rotation matrix in RoPE is data-independent and only a function of the relative position (i.e., R applied i − j times), which limits its expressivity; indeed, recent work [7] demonstrates that RoPE-based transformers are still computationally constrained to the TC0 complexity class, the complexity class of ordinary transformers with absolute position embeddings [46]. As a potential consequence, RoPE-based transformers have been empirically found to have difficulty with simple synthetic tasks that require a form of sequential reasoning, such as flip-flop language modeling [38] and certain state-tracking tasks [48]. Insofar as such simple sequential reasoning underlie real-world capabilities that we want

The implementation of the PaTH attention layer is also made available as part of the FLASHLINEARATTENTION library [78, 77]: https://github.com/fla-org/flash-linear-attention

in our LLMs, these failure modes highlight the need to design new primitives that can overcome these theoretical and empirical limitations of existing attention layers.

This paper develops PaTH, a position encoding scheme with accumulated Householder transformations, targeting the above problem. In PaTH, the attention logit is still parameterized i Hijkj, but the matrix Hij ∈ Rd×d is obtained via a cumulative product as a bilinear form q⊤ of data-dependent matrices along the path between positions j and i, where the matrices have Householder-like identity-plus-rank-one structure. Intuitively, this formulation captures the cumula- tive transformation between positions, enabling PaTH to dynamically adapt to input data and solve certain state-tracking problems. Indeed, we show that a constant-layer PaTH-based transformer can solve an NC1-complete problem under AC0 reductions, i.e., PaTH can extend transformers beyond the TC0 complexity class (assuming TC0 ̸= NC1).

To scale up PaTH Attention, we develop a FlashAttention-like algorithm [14] for hardware-efficient parallel training that exploits a compact representation for products of Householder matrices [5, 24]. Empirical experiments demonstrate that PaTH-based can solve challenging synthetic state-tracking tasks where RoPE-based transformers struggle. On moderate-scale language modeling with 760M- parameter transformers, we show that PaTH can improve upon baselines such as RoPE as well as the Forgetting Transformer [36], a recent baseline which modulates the attention logits via a data- dependent additive term. We find that combining the two approaches results in further improvements, and models trained this way generalize well beyond the training sequence length.

Headline: MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab Unveils PaTH Attention: Data-Dependent Positional Encoding to Overcome Transformer Memory Bottlenecks

News Article:

The MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab has announced a breakthrough in Large Language Model (LLM) architecture with the introduction of "PaTH Attention," marking a substantial shift away from the industry-standard Rotary Position Encoding (RoPE). This development directly addresses a central bottleneck in artificial intelligence—the persistent inability of transformers to accurately track internal states and variables across extended sequences. The new approach introduces a dynamic, data-dependent mechanism for position encoding, fundamentally revising how models understand and utilize sequence order in structured domains such as language.

Rotary Position Encoding (RoPE) is the prevailing standard for injecting positional information into transformer-based architectures. Its mathematical core is a data-independent rotation, parameterized solely by the relative position between sequence elements. In RoPE, transformations applied to keys and queries during attention computation are fixed functions of positional difference, implemented via block-diagonal rotation matrices. This design enables computational efficiency and has been instrumental in the practical scaling of LLMs. However, it is fundamentally permutation-invariant, providing no mechanism for the model to adapt its notion of position based on sequence content. As documented in the technical literature, this rigidity imposes theoretical limitations on expressivity—RoPE-based transformers remain constrained to the TC0 complexity class and empirically struggle with tasks demanding sequential reasoning or robust state-tracking, such as flip-flop language modeling and stateful sequence manipulations ([Abstract], [Introduction], [Citation]).

The introduction of PaTH (Position Encoding via Accumulating Householder Transformations) is a direct response to these limitations. PaTH deploys a position encoding scheme predicated on the cumulative product of input-dependent, Householder-like transformations. Concretely, for two sequence positions \( i \) and \( j \), the attention logit is formulated as a bilinear form \( q_i^\top H_{ij} k_j \), where \( H_{ij} \) is the cumulative product of data-dependent matrices along the path from \( j \) to \( i \). Each matrix in the product is structured as an identity-plus-rank-one (i.e., Householder-like) transformation, allowing the system to encode and adapt positional representations in relation to the actual input at each position ([Abstract], [Introduction], [Citation]).

This data-dependent approach institutes what researchers characterize as 'positional memory'—a transformative capability where the sequence of accumulated transformations directly reflects the processed content, not merely the static location within the sequence. As a word or token is processed, the resulting Householder transformation is a function of that token, enabling the context to modulate the path encoding in real time. This mechanism equips PaTH-based transformers with the capacity to model and track internal states as they evolve across complex input structures. The architectural innovation resolves previously intractable challenges for state-tracking and sequential reasoning in transformer models, as benchmarked against synthetic and real-world sequence modeling tasks ([Abstract], [Real-World Anchor Event]).

The theoretical impact is pronounced: PaTH-based transformers, employing a constant number of layers, demonstrably solve NC1-complete problems under AC0 reductions—a result that positions the architecture above RoPE (and related position encoding schemes) in the hierarchy of computational expressivity. Practically, empirical results show that PaTH markedly outperforms RoPE and recent baselines such as the Forgetting Transformer in both synthetic and moderate-scale language modeling tasks, achieving improvements in both target benchmarks and generalization to extended sequence lengths ([Abstract], [Introduction]). Furthermore, the combination of PaTH with established techniques, such as modulating attention logits with data-dependent additive terms, yields additive benefits, suggesting substantial broad applicability ([Abstract]).

To enable the scaling of PaTH attention to hardware-efficient parallel training, the researchers devised a FlashAttention-style algorithm that exploits the compactness inherent in products of Householder matrices. This algorithm minimizes input/output (I/O) cost and is implemented within the FLASHLINEARATTENTION library, publicly available to facilitate further research and application ([Introduction], [Citation]). The practicality of the method—combining theoretical advances with implementation efficiency—underscores its potential as a new primitive for next-generation language models and AI systems.

The MIT-IBM announcement signals a pivotal shift for the technical community. As transformers underpin an expanding array of agentic workflows and logic-intensive AI applications—including multi-step reasoning, advanced code debugging, and persistent state management—the PaTH architecture provides a new solution to memory and state-tracking constraints previously unmet by RoPE-based LLMs ([Real-World Anchor Event]).

For computer science researchers and practitioners seeking to advance the state of large-scale language modeling and sequence reasoning, direct engagement with the PaTH Attention research is highly recommended. Full implementation resources and technical details are accessible in the original paper ([Citation]) and the accompanying FLASHLINEARATTENTION library.

Access the research paper and code at: https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.16381 and https://github.com/fla-org/flash-linear-attention

anchor_event.full_content:
At the heart of the MIT-IBM breakthrough is a departure from Rotary Position Encoding (RoPE), the current gold standard used by almost all major AI labs. While RoPE allows models to understand the relative distance between words, it is "data-independent," meaning the way a model perceives position is fixed regardless of what the text actually says. The PaTH architecture—short for Position Encoding via Accumulating Householder Transformations—replaces these static rotations with content-aware reflections. As the model reads a sequence, each word produces a unique "Householder transformation" that adjusts the model’s internal state, effectively creating a path of accumulated memory that evolves with the context.

The MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab has announced a fundamental breakthrough in Large Language Model (LLM) architecture that addresses one of the most persistent bottlenecks in artificial intelligence: the inability of models to accurately track internal states and variables over long sequences. Known as "PaTH Attention," this new architecture replaces the industry-standard position encoding used by models like GPT-4 with a dynamic, data-dependent mechanism that allows AI to maintain a "positional memory" of every word and action it processes.

This development, finalized in late 2025 and showcased at recent major AI conferences, represents a significant leap in "expressive" AI. By moving beyond the mathematical limitations of current Transformers, the researchers have created a framework that can solve complex logic and state-tracking problems—such as debugging thousands of lines of code or managing multi-step agentic workflows—that were previously thought to be computationally impossible for standard LLMs. The announcement marks a pivotal moment for **IBM ([NYSE: IBM](https://markets.financialcontent.com/stocks/quote?Symbol=NY%3AIBM))** as it seeks to redefine the technical foundations of enterprise-grade AI.

title: Helping AI agents search to get the best results out of large language models
abstract: We introduce a new approach to agent programming, the development of LLM- based agents. Current approaches to agent programming often entangle two aspects of agent design: the core workflow logic and the inference-time strategy (e.g., tree search). We introduce probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN), a programming model that disentangles these two concerns, allowing the programmer to describe the agent workflow and independently experiment with different inference-time strategies by simply changing a few inputs. We provide an implementation of PAN in Python as the ENCOMPASS framework, which uses a Python decorator to compile agent workflow programs into a search space. We present three case studies that demonstrate how the framework lets the programmer quickly improve the reliability of an agent and easily switch between different inference-time strategies, all with little additional coding.
introduction:
Introduction

Recent work has shown the power of scaling inference-time compute for LLMs [1, 2], where popular strategies include best-of-N sampling [3, 4, 5], refinement [6, 7], and tree search [8, 9]. In LLM-based agents — systems that define how LLMs and other components interact to solve a task — these same strategies have become common ways of improving performance and reliability. Furthermore, several works have demonstrated the utility of applying sophisticated search and backtracking strategies in AI agents to improve performance in various tasks [8, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14].

While various frameworks have been developed to simplify the low-level interaction between the program and the LLM [15, 16, 17, 18], a framework for agent inference-time strategies has been absent. Our goal is to develop an inference-time strategy framework: a framework that makes it easy to experiment with different inference-time strategies independently of the design and implementation of the underlying agent workflow. Such a framework is intended not to replace, but to be used in conjunction with LLM prompting and tool use frameworks, such as LangChain [15] or DSPy [16].

We target “program-in-control” style agents, where one defines the workflow in code and uses the LLM to accomplish specific subtasks [14, 19, 20, 21, 22].2 In these agents, inference-time strategies have traditionally been limited to sampling and refinement loops [6, 7, 19, 20], whereas more sophisticated strategies such as beam search and tree search have been rarely explored [14].

∗Work performed as a consultant for Asari AI 2This “program-in-control” style contrasts with the “LLM-in-control” style where the LLM decides the full

sequence of operations (tool calls) in the workflow [8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13].

Figure 1: An ENCOMPASS program specifies an agent workflow, which is compiled into a search space object, and inference-time scaling is accomplished through search over the nondeterministic execution paths of the agent workflow.

We identify the key bottleneck to be the entanglement of the inference-time scaling strategy with the core workflow logic when programming the agent. Programmers typically bake the inference- time strategy into the agent workflow [14, 19, 20, 22], which is inflexible, reduces readability, and limits the kinds of inference-time strategies that can be easily implemented. Therefore, we aim to design a framework that cleanly separates the representation of the core workflow logic from the inference-time scaling strategy. The programmer could then make minimal modifications to their agent to flexibly experiment with different inference-time strategies. Also, different agents would no longer require custom implementations of the same inference-time strategy, but can instead reuse a common implementation.

Our key insight is that inference-time strategies can be viewed as instances of search over different execution paths of a nondeterministic program. We developed the ENCOMPASS Python programming framework (“enhancing agents with compiled agent search”), depicted in Figure 1. Figure 1a and Figure 1b show an agent program and its corresponding workflow, respectively. The user specifies the “locations of unreliability” in their agent source code using branchpoint() statements. A location of unreliability is an operation such as an LLM call where repeated invocations produce outputs of varying quality. Since these different outputs give rise to multiple possible futures of the program’s execution, the program has a tree of possible execution paths. ENCOMPASS compiles the program into a search space object (Figure 1c) so that search can be conducted over this tree of execution paths to find the path with the highest score (Figure 1d). We call this programming model probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN). As a form of angelic nondeterminism [23], PAN lets the programmer write their program pretending the unreliable operations always produce good outputs, and the runtime searches the space of possible execution paths for one where the operations indeed produced good outputs.

Our work makes the following concrete contributions:

We introduce the PAN programming model (Section 2.1), which uses angelic nondetermin- ism to separate inference-time algorithms (search policy) from the underlying logic of the agent (specification of the search space).

We present ENCOMPASS, a Python library that implements PAN (Section 2.2), providing 1. primitives like branchpoint() that the programmer can use inside their ENCOMPASS function, 2. a Python function decorator that compiles an ENCOMPASS function into a search space object at run-time, and 3. common search algorithms, as well as an interface for implementing custom search algorithms.

We illustrate how ENCOMPASS provides a unifying framework for common inference-time strategies and agentic patterns, which are special cases of search over nondeterministic execution paths of ENCOMPASS programs (Section 3). ENCOMPASS also provides a natural generalization of these inference-time strategies.

We present case studies showing how ENCOMPASS enables easy experimentation of various inference-time search strategies over an underlying agent workflow, allowing one to quickly identify the best-performing strategy (Section 4). ENCOMPASS opens up new possibilities for inference-time scaling of program-in-control style agents, where inference-time strategies that were previously considered too cumbersome to implement are now made possible by ENCOMPASS.

Headline:  
ENCOMPASS Framework Unveils Plug-and-Play Search Strategies for LLM-Based Agents through Probabilistic Angelic Nondeterminism

News Article:

This capability is achieved by decoupling the agent workflow logic from the inference-time search strategy, realized through the programming model of probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN). As described in the project’s release, this technical separation enables developers to rapidly iterate, insert, or modify search strategies across a fixed agent workflow, drastically decreasing the code required to incorporate advanced search and promoting the identification of strategies that maximize agent performance ([Real-World Anchor Event], [Abstract], [Introduction]).

This modular approach addresses a persistent bottleneck in agent programming: the entanglement of workflow logic and inference-time scaling strategies. Traditionally, the inference-time strategy for LLM-based agents—often implemented as best-of-N sampling, tree search, or refinement algorithms—has been deeply integrated within the core agent code ([Introduction]). Such monolithic design not only impedes agent readability and adaptability but also renders the experimentation and reuse of search strategies burdensome, with each agent requiring a custom implementation for comparable search behavior.

ENCOMPASS offers a formal resolution to this challenge by introducing PAN, in which unreliable operations—identified by the programmer with PAN’s branchpoint() primitive—delineate explicit locations of nondeterminism within the agent workflow ([Abstract], [Introduction]). At these points, the outcome of an LLM call or other non-deterministic operation may generate a tree of candidate execution paths, each potentially leading to a different program outcome. The ENCOMPASS Python decorator compiles such a marked agent workflow into a search space object, enabling inference-time search over the entire execution path tree without altering the underlying workflow logic.

The direct benefit of ENCOMPASS’s separation—enabled by probabilistic angelic nondeterminism—is threefold: rapid experimentation, reduced coding effort, and seamless optimization of agentic strategies for maximizing performance. As explicitly demonstrated by the ENCOMPASS framework, experimenters can “plug-and-play a few common search strategies… or define their own custom strategy,” applying search methods such as Monte Carlo tree search or beam search to the same workflow with only minor adjustments to input parameters ([Real-World Anchor Event]). According to lead author Zhening Li, the approach “lets programmers easily experiment with different search strategies to find the one that makes the AI agent perform the best.” The case studies presented confirm that even advanced search strategies—previously considered cumbersome to implement—can be deployed or swapped with minimal code modifications ([Abstract], [Introduction]). Thus, the separation realized by PAN tangibly lowers the barrier for agent developers to experiment with and ultimately deploy the most effective inference-time search configurations for their desired performance metrics.

In the context of LLM-based agents following the “program-in-control” paradigm—where a Python-coded workflow orchestrates LLM invocations for discrete subtasks—ENCOMPASS generalizes and unifies previously ad hoc inference-time scaling techniques ([Introduction]). Search strategies, which were typically limited to ad hoc sampling and basic refinement, are now special cases of search over nondeterministic execution paths within ENCOMPASS’s compiled search spaces. The facility to overlay and generalize these search strategies opens up expanded possibilities for reliability tuning, task completion guarantees, and benchmarking of agentic performance across architectures.

Practically, ENCOMPASS’s design means that developers targeting reliability or task success in LLM agents can specify agent workflows in standard Python and later overlay different search or backtracking strategies through decorator and input adjustments, sidestepping the overhead of bespoke search code for each agent. This modular composition aligns with research trends emphasizing the importance of inference-time compute scaling in boosting LLM agent reliability ([Abstract], [Introduction]), while avoiding architectural rigidity.

The ENCOMPASS release is thus poised not only to accelerate iterative research on agentic search strategies, but also to reduce maintenance burden and encourage architectural experimentation in LLM-based agent development. As the ENCOMPASS source code and case studies become available, the research community is encouraged to evaluate its potential for unifying and advancing the landscape of agent programming and inference-time optimization strategies.

For technical details, implementation paradigms, and in-depth case study results, access the full ENCOMPASS paper at [https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2512.03571](https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2512.03571).

anchor_event.summary: MIT CSAIL and Asari AI have launched the ENCOMPASS framework, a novel system that separates agent workflow design from inference-time search strategy, enabling AI agents to reliably search for optimal outputs through backtracking and search space exploration. This advancement lets developers optimize and experiment with workflows independently of search algorithms, resulting in significant performance gains for LLM-based agents.
anchor_event.full_content:
Users can also plug-and-play a few common search strategies provided by EnCompass out of the box, or define their own custom strategy. For example, you could opt for Monte Carlo tree search, which builds a search tree by balancing exploration and exploitation, or beam search, which keeps the best few outputs from every step. EnCompass makes it easy to experiment with different approaches to find the best strategy to maximize the likelihood of successfully completing your task.

“With EnCompass, we’ve separated the search strategy from the underlying workflow of an AI agent,” says lead author Zhening Li ’25, MEng ’25, who is an MIT electrical engineering and computer science (EECS) PhD student, CSAIL researcher, and research consultant at Asari AI. “Our framework lets programmers easily experiment with different search strategies to find the one that makes the AI agent perform the best.”

So just how code-efficient is EnCompass for adding search to agent programs? According to researchers’ findings, the framework drastically cut down how much programmers needed to add to their agent programs to add search, helping them experiment with different strategies to find the one that performs the best.

title: One pull of a string is all it takes to deploy these complex structures
abstract: We present a computational approach for designing freeform structures that can be rapidly assembled from initially flat configurations by a single string pull. The target structures are decomposed into rigid spatially varied quad tiles that are optimized to approximate the user-provided surface, forming a flat mechanical linkage. Our algorithm then uses a two-step method to find a physically realizable string path that controls only a subset of tiles to smoothly actuate the structure from flat to assembled configuration. We initially compute the minimal subset of tiles that are required to be controlled with the string considering the geometry of the structure and interaction among the tiles. We then find a valid string path through these tiles that minimizes friction, which will assemble the flat linkage into the target 3D structure upon tightening a single string. The resulting designs can be easily manufactured with computational fabrication techniques such as 3D printing, CNC milling, molding, etc. in flat configuration that, in addition to manufacturing, facilitates storage and transportation. We validate our approach by developing a series of physical prototypes and showcasing various application case studies, ranging from medical devices, space shelters, to architectural designs.
introduction: The construction of deployable structures from flat modular piecessimplifies both fabrication and assembly on site while reducing thetransportation and storage requirements. By combining rigidity andflexibility, these structures find applications in fields ranging fromarchitecture [Panetta et al. 2019] to electronics in robotics [Chen et al.2024; Liu et al . 2024] and biomechanical devices [Wang et al. 2023b].Despite this prominent progress, achieving freeform deployablesthat integrate both modularization and effective actuation remainsan open challenge.Deployables have recently gained significant traction in com-putational design and fabrication, yet existing actuation strategiesstruggle to achieve reversibility, scalability, and reliable supportfor shell-like, doubly curved geometries. Some of the existing ap-proaches exploring reversible and scalable strategies face limitationswhile approximating geometries with variation of sign in the meancurvature (e.g., negative mean curvature [Konaković-Luković et al .ACM Trans. Graph., Vol. 44, No. 6, Article 234. Publication date: December 2025.234:2 • Zaman, Aslarus, Li, Mueller, Konaković Luković2018]), and they often require specialized equipment to actuate orrisk potential jamming [Eguchi et al. 2022]. A method that unifiessurface rationalization with optimized actuation enables structuresto transition from flat to freeform configurations and allows fast,repeatable deployment as well as disassembly. This capability opensnew opportunities across domains and scales, ranging from novel de-signs of compact, transportable biomedical devices, foldable robots,and consumer items (such as chairs or bike helmets) to large-scaleportable space habitats and rapidly deployable emergency sheltersor field hospitals during floods or earthquakes.We introduce a new method for deploying a structure from theflat configuration to the assembled curved configuration with onlya single pull of a string (see Figure 1). Our approach is based onthe planar linkage of spatially varying quadrilateral tiles whichare connected by rotational hinges at the vertices. The string thenpasses through only a small subset of tiles and lifts the structuresimply by tightening it. This setup allows for reversible actuation:if the string is released, the structure returns to a flat configuration.The structure requires a simple unidirectional pulling mechanism,making it independent of the scale. Several key contributions enableour method:• We approximate a user-provided structure by modularizingit into scale-varying quadrilateral frustums and derive a flatconfiguration by computing hinge placements that respectgeometric curvature, forming a flat mechanical linkage.• We present a general analysis showing that closing the bound-ary gaps alone suffices to guide all linkage tiles into the de-sired 3D configuration.• We identify the minimum set of lift points for assembling thefinal structure and optimize the string routing to minimizechannel friction. This effectively reduces the total resistiveforce during actuation of the structure from a flat to assembledconfiguration.• Our method supports reversible actuation on a wide scalerange, as demonstrated by proof-of-concept prototypes acrossvarious domains and scale. We show examples lifted by hu-man hand, robotic arms, and architectural cranes.Our method offers several key advantages beyond its ability tohandle both flat fabrication and complex curvature. Since all tilesremain fully enclosed and connected through a single low-frictionstring path, the structure is simple to deploy, disassemble, and reuse.By eliminating the need for additional scaffolding or sealed layers,users also benefit from lower material costs and faster productiontimes, an attribute crucial for emergency shelters, large-scale tem-porary architectural installations, and consumer products alike. Wedemonstrate potential applications throughout this work, rangingfrom compact medical wearables (e.g., a foldable backrest and footsplint, see Figure 2) to consumer items (e.g., foldable chair, see Fig-ure 12), crane-lifted architectural structures ( see Figure 17), andtransportable space habitats (see Figure 16). Our method also has thepotential to facilitate robotic assembly by replacing computationally-intensive and challenging assembly including individual block de-tection and motion planning of each block with the far simplertask of detecting and pulling a single string (See Figure 15 andSupplementary Video, 01:23).Fig. 2. Application in personalized medical items such as splints (Left) andposture correctors (Right). Both are optimized for a given patient, thenmanufactured, stored and carried flat, and can be easily assembled by asingle string pull.The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2discusses relevant papers and their connection to our work. Section 3provides an outline of our method. Section 4 provides the designframework for the flat configuration and the placement of the hinges.Section 5 describes the string route finder algorithm. Section 6discusses fabrication of the prototypes and application case studies.Section 7 highlights the limitations of our approach, and identifiespotential future research directions.

Headline:  
MIT Engineers Unveil Single-String Deployable Structures: A Computational Advance with Far-Reaching Implications for Rapid Assembly

News Article:  

MIT engineers have recently showcased a striking advance in deployable architecture: a system that encodes three-dimensional structures into flat panels, which then transform with the pull of a single string ([1]). A mechanism both elegant and robust, the system enables flat-pack components to smoothly unfurl into predetermined forms after a solitary and directed actuation event—opening new technical frontiers in manufacturing, transportation, and disaster response infrastructure.

This mechanism is underpinned by a new computational method that formalizes the design and deployment of freeform structures from an initially flat configuration ([Abstract], [Introduction], [Citation]). Central to this method is the decomposition of any target geometry into rigid, spatially varied quad tiles, interconnected by mechanical linkages at the vertices. This modularization facilitates fabrication of the structure in a flat state using techniques such as 3D printing, CNC milling, or molding, inherently simplifying logistics of storage and delivery ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

Critically, the assembly process is controlled by a single string, which is routed through a geometric subset of these modular tiles. Upon pulling the string, the system actuates, transforming the linkage from its flat configuration to its final three-dimensional form—achieving both reversibility and scalability ([Abstract], [Introduction]). If the string is released, the system reverts to its compact, flat-packed state. The resulting workflow enables not only rapid and repeatable deployment, but also reduces friction and resistive forces during actuation by algorithmically optimizing both the sequence and path of the string ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

**Manufacturing: Streamlined, Modular Production**

The implications for manufacturing are immediate and technically significant. Flat fabrication of modular quad tiles with precise hinge placement circumvents the need for complex, volumetric molds or multi-stage assembly ([Abstract]). For custom consumer products such as foldable chairs or configurable furniture, this approach leverages computational rationalization of surfaces to generate scalable quad tiling that can be manufactured en masse, compactly stored, and shipped flat ([Introduction]). The encoded mechanical linkage and single-string actuation redefine the assembly process: production moves from labor-intensive multi-component joining to a unidirectional actuation event that requires minimal training or tools ([Abstract], [Introduction]). In proof-of-concept prototypes, both manual (human hand) and automated (robotic arm, architectural cranes) agents have successfully deployed structures using this string-driven protocol, highlighting the method’s adaptability across manufacturing modalities and size scales ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

**Transportation: Compact, Efficient Logistics**

The capacity to transport large-scale architectural or functional structures in flat-packed states marks a breakthrough for logistical efficiency. For applications such as portable space habitats or emergency shelters, the ability to ship rigid, quad-tiled panels at a fraction of their deployed volume (and with reduced weight) mitigates perennial challenges in aerospace cargo or field logistics ([Introduction]; see also Fig. 16-17). On-site, the transformation from storage to use is governed by a fast, robust, and reversible actuation—realized by a single string pull—bypassing the need for specialized assembly crews or alignment jigs. This reduction in assembly complexity and volumetric footprint could directly benefit urban planning, disaster relief, or temporary event architectures by compressing deployment windows and material requirements.

**Emergency Response: Rapid, Reversible Infrastructure**

Perhaps the domain of greatest immediate impact is emergency response. Field hospitals, flood shelters, or temporary command centers can be pre-fabricated in modular, flat-packed arrays, shipped to disaster zones, and deployed with minimal technical skill ([Introduction]). The mechanical linkage’s reversibility—structures can be disassembled by releasing the string—enables repeated use across incidents or rapid repurposing in the field. Medical devices, such as personalized splints or posture correctors, capitalize on the same design principle: custom profiles are engineered as quad-tiled surfaces and manufactured flat, then actuated into patient-specific geometries at the point of care ([Introduction], Fig. 2). This methodology not only decreases material and storage costs, but also accelerates response timelines in critical environments.

**Bridging Technical Innovation and Real-World Applications**

The MIT system’s technical virtues—a unified approach to surface rationalization, actuation via optimized string routing, and freeform geometric support—integrate with the broader landscape of computational fabrication and move the boundary of what is possible with deployable systems ([Abstract], [Introduction]). By eliminating the need for additional scaffolding, specialized actuators, or sealed volumetric layers, the method catalyzes new design paradigms, compressing both the capital and temporal costs associated with physical infrastructure.

These mechanistic breakthroughs—rooted in verified algorithmic strategies for minimal lift point identification, string path optimization, and modular design—redefine established workflows across multiple domains ([Abstract]). The spectrum of demonstrated applications, spanning foldable medical devices, consumer goods, crane-lifted architectural pavilions, and space-bound habitats, validates the protocol’s versatility and sets a research agenda for further exploration of robotic deployment and computationally optimized structural assembly ([Introduction]).

For researchers, engineers, and practitioners eager to examine the underlying algorithms, mechanical models, and comprehensive case studies driving this innovation, the source paper (https://dx.doi.org/10.1145/3763357) is indispensable. Accessing this work will provide the technical depth required to leverage and adapt the single-string deployable paradigm across emerging challenges in computational design and rapid infrastructure deployment.
