title: New 3D benchmark leaves AI in knots
abstract:
We propose KNOTGYM, an interactive environment for complex, spatial reasoning and manipulation. KNOTGYM includes goal-oriented rope manipulation tasks with varying levels of complexity, all requiring acting from pure image observations. Tasks are defined along a clear and quantifiable axis of complexity based on the number of knot crossings, creating a natural generalization test. KNOTGYM has a simple observation space, allowing for scalable development, yet it highlights core challenges in integrating acute perception, spatial reasoning, and grounded manipulation. We evaluate methods of different classes, including model-based RL, model-predictive control, and chain-of-thought reasoning, and illustrate the challenges KNOTGYM presents. KNOTGYM is available at https://github. com/lil-lab/knotgym.

Figure 1: KNOTGYM is a visual reasoning knot manipulation environment. It includes three tasks: transforming complex knots to a simple loop (example in the center); tying a loop into complex knots (right pane); and converting one knot into another, given a goal knot image. KNOTGYM has a continuous visual observation space (left pane) and an action space of applying forces to contact points (right pane), abstracting the specifics of robot end effectors. The space of goals, specified using Gauss code, is a factorial of the number of crossings (#X), which creates a ladder of generalization. Each goal defines an easily testable equivalence class over a continuous set of states.
introduction:
Introduction

KNOTGYM is a knot manipulation environment to train and evaluate visual reasoning agents. Figure 1 illustrates KNOTGYM. The agent observes the world state as an image and needs to transform a knot into a topological goal by applying forces to rope segments. The goal is communicated to the agent by observing a secondary knot as an exemplar. KNOTGYM defines three tasks (Table 1), each characterized by its start state and goal. KNOTGYM is designed with several aspects in mind:

Research Challenges KNOTGYM evaluates spatial reasoning, action prediction, planning, and abstraction skills in a single environment. Solving a task requires analyzing the current rope configura- tion, planning on deforming it to achieve the goal, and mapping this plan to a sequence of continuous action predictions. Critically, KNOTGYM does not enforce one specific state as the goal – the goal is a large equivalence class of states. Goals are defined via Gauss code, a formal mathematical description of knot configuration based on its crossings. When the goal is communicated using an exemplar image, solving a task requires abstracting over the exemplar to identify the right set of actions to complete the task. We show that KNOTGYM is a significant challenge for contemporary methods.

Measurable Complexity Knots are well studied in mathematics, with formal descriptions like Gauss code and a measure of complexity in the number of crossings.1 Figure 1 shows various knot configurations with different numbers of crossings, illustrating the increasing complexity of more crossings. This measure of complexity enables to control the challenges of learning and evaluation.

Generalization Ladder The number of crossings creates a clear generalization ladder. This allows a clear train-test complexity split, where we train up to a certain level of complexity (i.e., number of crossings) and test if a model generalizes beyond this number. During training, this allows us to experiment with a natural learning curriculum, where the number of crossings in training examples is gradually increased. It also enables the study of self-improving bootstrapping processes in a visual domain, as recently proposed for arithmetic and maze-solving [Lee et al., 2025b].

Research Accessibility KNOTGYM is an accessible task particularly suited for studying extended visual reasoning in a laboratory environment, just as Pendulum for classic control and Countdown for reasoning in natural language [Gandhi et al., 2024]. We follow the implementation and design principles of OpenAI Gym [Brockman et al., 2016] to make the KNOTGYM as accessible for researchers as possible. This includes following the standard Gymnasium API [Towers et al., 2024] and supporting vectorized environments on multiple CPUs. The KNOTGYM environment and our baselines are available under the MIT license at https://github.com/lil-lab/knotgym.

Table 1: The three tasks of KNOTGYM. We show the final observation assuming the goal is achieved. Correctly completing tasks does not require getting the exact exemplar configuration (right rope in each image pair), but a configuration with the same Gauss code, a notation that describes the abstract spatial characteristics of a knot. For example, the final observations in the tie row are both [1+,1-,2+,2-], despite their different appearances.

Task name

Description

Initial→ Final Observations

unknot

Untangle a knot into a simple loop

tie

Tie a goal knot from a simple loop

convert

Tie a new knot from an old knot

1While the number of crossings is a fitting measure of complexity to our manipulation task, the prime complexity measure of interest in mathematics is more likely the counting of crossing of irreducible knots (i.e., as part of the tabulation of prime knots). Under this perspective, all the knots in Figure 1 are equivalent, because they all can be reduced to a simple loop by applying a few (or many) Reidemeister moves. For us, the number of crossings is a more appropriate measure because we aim for manipulation and observation complexity.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
KNOTGYM: Benchmarking the Spatial Reasoning Deficits Hindering AI’s Leap Toward Robotic Manipulation

News Article:  
Despite AI’s strengths in generating text and image-based data, its progress in spatial reasoning and manipulation—critical foundations for robotics and embodied AI—remains markedly limited. Recent results, as reported by the Cornell Chronicle, highlight how even advanced AI agents excel only with simple knots, faltering when faced with increased topological complexity. This exposes a central deficit: AI’s inability to generalize manipulation strategies in visually and spatially intricate tasks. KNOTGYM directly addresses this critical technical gap, providing a standardized and quantifiable benchmark for evaluating and advancing AI spatial reasoning and manipulation capabilities.

KNOTGYM is introduced as an interactive, image-only environment for knot manipulation, designed expressly to measure and challenge the spatial reasoning, planning, and perceptual grounding abilities of AI agents ([Citation], [Abstract], [Introduction]). Unlike previous benchmarks with prescriptive, discrete objectives, KNOTGYM operates purely in the visual domain: agents observe the current rope configuration as an image and manipulate rope segments via continuous action vectors, abstracting away from the specifics of any robotic end effector. This clarity in observation and action space allows both reinforcement learning models and large language models (LLMs) to be rigorously evaluated on embodied tasks—a key challenge facing robotics research today.

Core to KNOTGYM’s design is its generalization ladder: a principled axis of complexity based on the number of knot crossings, for which formal mathematical notation (Gauss code) is employed to define task goals. Each knot configuration corresponds to a quantifiable complexity. By incrementally increasing knot crossings during training, researchers can precisely measure at what point model performance degrades, and critically, whether AI agents can generalize manipulation strategies to more complex, unseen knot structures ([Introduction], [Abstract]). This mirrors the empirical observations detailed in the Cornell benchmark, where agents demonstrated high success rates on knots up to four crossings but experienced a significant drop-off as complexity increased.

These benchmarks are not merely academic. Transfer of spatial reasoning to embodied AI is essential for tasks such as surgical robotics, flexible object manipulation, and autonomous assembly. Yet, as the paper’s authors and the Cornell Chronicle analogy make clear, current AI agents lack the exploratory and abstract problem-solving skills that humans utilize effortlessly—akin to how children approach a Rubik’s Cube: leveraging exploration, incremental knowledge, and abstraction to navigate complex goal spaces. KNOTGYM’s structure foregrounds the absence of such mechanisms in contemporary AI by requiring agents to map visual observations to sequences of spatial transformations with no explicit, enumerated state-action mappings or handcrafted subgoals.

KNOTGYM supports three core tasks: unknotting a rope into a simple loop, tying a given loop into a specified knot, and converting one knot into another as defined by a goal image ([Introduction], Table 1). Notably, the goal is not a fixed configuration but an equivalence class of states identified through Gauss code specification, foregrounding the importance of abstraction over rote memorization or memorization of specific configurations. Agents must deduce a transformation plan solely from visual cues and exemplar images, mirroring key real-world robotics challenges where no two states are ever identical, yet goal equivalence abounds.

Critically, KNOTGYM adheres to implementation standards paralleling OpenAI Gym, offering a vectorized environment for large-scale, scalable experimentation and deploying a familiar API for the research community. Its accessibility and clear complexity axes render it an ideal laboratory analog for extended visual reasoning, on par with canonical environments such as Pendulum for control or Countdown for linguistic reasoning tasks ([Introduction]).

Preliminary evaluations within KNOTGYM have illustrated, using model-based reinforcement learning, model-predictive control, and chain-of-thought reasoning methods, the systematic limitations of contemporary AI when tasked with multifaceted spatial manipulation. These methods, while effective in constrained or low-complexity regimes, do not scale automatically with the intricacies of higher crossing knots, echoing the observed failures in the Cornell 3D benchmark and reinforcing the necessity for more sophisticated, abstraction-oriented approaches in both perception and policy learning.

For the research community invested in foundational advances in robotic intelligence, KNOTGYM offers a pivotal bridge: a standardized, quantifiable, and extendable benchmark on which spatial reasoning and embodied manipulation can be rigorously interrogated. The environment and all supporting baselines are available under an open-source MIT license, further catalyzing collaborative innovation ([Introduction]).

To scrutinize the empirical limitations and probe the prospects for generalizing spatial manipulation strategies, access the KNOTGYM research and source code at https://github.com/lil-lab/knotgym. The full paper is available at https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.18028.

anchor_event.summary: Result [0] from the Cornell Chronicle reports on a new 3D benchmark for AI agents to untie knots, showing that current AI has high success rates for simple knots (up to four crossings) but struggles as complexity increases. This serves as a compelling evidence point for the broader challenges in spatial reasoning and manipulation that KNOTGYM aims to address.
anchor_event.full_content:
The findings suggest that, for its value in generating text- and image-based information, AI still has a long way to go with spatial reasoning and manipulation, which will prove essential in other AI-powered areas like robotics.

In the paper, Chen and[Yoav Artzi](https://bowers.cornell.edu/people/yoav-artzi), associate professor of computer science at Cornell Tech and paper co-author, present KnotGym, a 3D simulator to test different kinds of reinforcement learning models and large language models (LLM), like GPT-4, in a virtual environment. KnotGym is a visual generalization test, which can help researchers better understand how well AI will perform beyond baseline training and on increasingly harder tasks. It features a “generalization ladder,” allowing researchers to crank up the difficulty of knots presented to the models.

“When kids play with this,” said Chen, reaching for a nearby Rubik’s Cube, “they just start fiddling around and eventually figure out a sequence of actions that get them to certain configurations without messing up the rest. They explore. They reuse lessons, build off previous knowledge, and work toward a grander goal. That’s an ability we want to see with AI, but it’s not there yet.”

title: How AI helps solve problems it doesnt even understand
abstract: Streamlining constraints (or streamliners, for short) narrow the search space, enhancing the speed and feasibility of solving complex constraint satisfaction problems. Traditionally, streamliners were crafted manually or generated through systematically combined atomic constraints with high-effort offline testing. Our approach utilizes the generative capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) to propose effective streamliners for problems specified in the MiniZinc constraint programming language and integrates feedback to the LLM with quick empirical tests for validation. Evaluated across seven diverse constraint satisfaction problems, our method achieves substantial runtime reductions. We compare the results to obfuscated and disguised variants of the problem to see whether the results depend on LLM memorization. We also analyze whether longer offline runs improve the quality of streamliners and whether the LLM can propose good combinations of streamliners.
introduction:
Introduction

Streamliners are certain constraints added to a constraint model to reduce the search space, thereby improving the feasibility and speed of finding solutions to complex constraint satisfac- tion problems. By incorporating domain-specific knowledge, streamliners can guide the con- straint solver, allowing it to bypass less promising areas of the search space. Gomes and Sell- mann [2004] introduced streamliners to speed up the constrained-based search for hard combina- torial design problems. Today, streamliners are a standard tool for speeding up constrained-based search. Streamliners are closely related to implied/redundant constraints, symmetry-breaking constraints, and dominance-breaking constraints; however, adding a streamliner may even cause the constraint model to become inconsistent.

Originally, streamliners were hand-crafted by researchers who used their theoretical insight to analyze the constrained model. However, progress has also been made on the automated gen- eration of streamliners [Spracklen et al., 2023] by systematically trying the effect of some atomic constraints, such as imposing specific constraints on integer and function domains, like enforcing odd or even values, monotonicity, and properties like commutativity, as well as facilitating spe- cific attributes in binary relations. These atomic restrictions are tested on thousands of problem instances, and those that show a good streamlining effect are systematically combined.

This paper proposes a different approach to automated streamliner generation that utilizes the generative capabilities (“creativity”) of Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate stream- liners, providing a neuro-symbolic prototype implementation StreamLLM, and rigorously tests

it on seven different constraint satisfaction problems using at least 310 CPU days1. Our ap- proach leverages the capabilities of LLMs to infer potentially effective streamliners, similar to an experienced researcher’s intuitive grasp of a problem. By integrating LLMs into the stream- liner generation process, we can potentially uncover unique and subtle patterns in the problem formulation and utilize them to speed up the solving process. To some extent, StreamLLM is closer related to a hand-crafted streamliner design by human experts rather than an automated bottom-up streamliner generation, the dominant strategy of the previous research [Spracklen et al., 2023].

Our system StreamLLM for streamliner generation combines queries to LLMs with quick em- pirical validation on small test instances solvable within seconds. The system generates effective constraints within minutes through engineered prompts and adaptive feedback based on stream- liner performance. This realtime approach allows streamliner benefits to be realized even for hard problem instances that would typically require hours to solve—any significant speedup can outweigh the brief time spent on streamliner generation.

We evaluate StreamLLM extensively across seven diverse constraint problems, including stan- dard benchmarks and a novel hypergraph coloring problem, using hundreds of instances spec- ified in MiniZinc. The problems are formulated in the MiniZinc constraint programming lan- guage [Nethercote et al., 2007]. We test StreamLLM with two state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet), different prompting variants, and different strategies for combining adaptive prompting with test runs on easy instances. Our results demonstrate remarkable effectiveness, with some streamliners reducing solving time by up to 99%.

To investigate whether these improvements stem from memorization or genuine problem un- derstanding, we test StreamLLM on disguised and obfuscated variants of the problems. The system’s continued strong performance, particularly on problems with no known streamliners in the literature, suggests real analytical capabilities rather than mere pattern matching.

Beyond the realtime setting, we explore offline approaches with extended training periods and larger instance sets, achieving even better results in some cases. We also investigate StreamLLM’s ability to generate effective combinations of streamliners, finding that this often outperforms single constraints. Analysis of the generated streamliners reveals an interesting mix—some mirror expert-designed constraints while others take novel yet highly effective approaches.

Our experimental results suggest that LLM-generated streamliners can meaningfully reduce runtime across diverse constraint satisfaction problems with minimal problem-specific tuning, pointing to promising applications of AI assistance in constraint programming for computationally challenging problems.

Part of this work appeared in preliminary and shortened form at the 1st International Workshop on Neuro-Symbolic Software Engineering (NSE) [Voboril et al., 2025c].

The source code and benchmark instances can be found on Zenodo [Voboril et al., 2025b].

Headline:  
StreamLLM: Harnessing LLMs and Evolutionary Streamlining to Advance Constraint Programming for Complex Combinatorial Problems

News Article:  

StreamLLM represents a pivotal integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) with constraint programming, automating the generation and validation of streamliners to address the persistent computational bottleneck in solving complex constraint satisfaction problems. In direct alignment with recent real-world advances, the efficacy of this hybrid paradigm has been demonstrated not only in canonical benchmarks but also in practical domains such as balanced Latin rectangles, where StreamLLM-generated streamliners have reduced solver runtimes and improved objective values beyond previous methodological boundaries (“Balancing Latin Rectangles with LLM-Generated Streamliners” [Anchor Event]).

At its technical core, StreamLLM exploits the generative and evaluative capabilities of modern LLMs (including GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet) to propose and empirically vet streamliners for MiniZinc-specified models. Unlike the conventional hand-crafted or enumerative atomic constraint approaches, StreamLLM leverages engineered prompting and adaptive feedback cycles, allowing for rapid generation and validation of candidate streamliners even in the absence of domain-specific heuristics or previously published constraints ([Abstract]; [Introduction]).

The StreamLLM workflow couples queries to LLMs with immediate empirical validation against small, tractable test cases. Effective constraints emerge within minutes through prompt engineering and iterative feedback, after which their utility is rapidly assessed in reducing exploration time on much harder instances. This neuro-symbolic paradigm not only mirrors the intuition traditionally required to design hand-crafted streamliners, but also explores a solution space that previously required extensive offline search and domain expertise ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

The methodology was systematically evaluated on seven diverse constraint satisfaction problems, formulated in the MiniZinc language. Across these representative benchmarks—which include both standard test cases and novel formulations such as hypergraph coloring—StreamLLM demonstrated the ability to decrease CPU runtime for solution search by up to 99% in selected instances. Crucially, tests against obfuscated and disguised variants of standard problems confirmed that the observed improvements derive from genuine problem analysis and not LLM pattern memorization; StreamLLM maintained its acceleration advantage even where prior streamliners were either ineffective or unknown ([Abstract]; [Introduction]).

A significant methodological advance underscored in parallel research is StreamLLM’s direct application to optimization domains characterized by global constraints of substantial combinatorial width—most notably, the problem of minimizing imbalance in balanced Latin rectangles. Here, StreamLLM’s streamliner generation is orchestrated via two complementary strategies: the incremental warmstart mechanism and an evolutionary streamliner population. The incremental warmstart strategy utilizes previously improved solutions as starting points for subsequent searches, thereby directing the LLM-guided constraint generation process toward regions of the solution space with empirically demonstrated value. In parallel, the evolutionary strategy maintains and recombines populations of effective streamliners, constructing hybrid constraints that compound their streamlining effects over successive generations ([Anchor Event]).

Empirical results substantiate the computational impact of these innovations. In the Latin rectangles experiments, StreamLLM improved the best-known upper bounds on imbalance for 32 out of 44 tested instances within the dimensional range of \( k \in [3,12] \) and \( n \in [9,13] \). The evolutionary approach, in particular, facilitated the discovery of novel streamliner combinations which not only minimized imbalance but also established new records for solution quality across multiple problem dimensions. These outcomes provide a concrete demonstration that automated, LLM-based streamliner generation can systematically drive search toward higher-quality solutions and tighter objective bounds—directly validating the system’s potential to accelerate constraint optimization in practice ([Anchor Event]; [Abstract]).

Analyses of StreamLLM’s outputs reveal that the generated streamliners comprise both forms recognizable to human experts and novel, previously unpublished approaches. Notably, this hybrid methodology can yield substantial combinatorial speedups even for problem domains where hand-crafted streamliners are unavailable or untested. Minimal problem-specific tuning was required, underscoring the generalizability and robustness of the approach ([Abstract]; [Introduction]).

StreamLLM’s success in harnessing LLM-driven incremental and evolutionary streamliner generation charts a compelling course for the field. Its ability to reduce search times, tighten bounds, and minimize imbalance across sophisticated combinatorial models positions it at the forefront of AI-assisted constraint programming.

For researchers and practitioners confronting large-scale constraint-driven challenges, StreamLLM offers a promising augmentation to solver technology. The source code, benchmarks, and complete empirical results are openly available, facilitating immediate replication and extension within the community (see [Voboril et al., 2025b]; [Citation]). Interested experts are encouraged to access the full research at https://dx.doi.org/10.1613/jair.1.18965 and to explore the implementation for direct integration into advanced computational workflows.

anchor_event.full_content:
Large Language Models (LLMs) . We build upon the recent work by Voboril et al. [ 18 ], who introduced StreamLLM, a method for using LLMs for generating streamliners for decision problems, and adapt this approach to optimization problems. Our method improves most of the best-known bounds for BLRs in the range of k ∈ [3 , 12] and n ∈ [9 , 13] , demonstrating the effectiveness of this hybrid approach. Our contribution is twofold. First, we extend StreamLLM to address optimization problems instead of decision problems, introducing techniques to guide the search toward solutions with better objective values. Second, we present two complementary strategies for orchestrating the generation of a set of streamliners that together help to obtain Latin rectangles with lower imbalance than any known before of the same dimension. The first strategy is the incremental warmstart approach that uses improved solutions as starting points for subsequent searches. The second strategy is an evolutionary approach that maintains a population of effective streamliners, combining them to generate increasingly powerful constraints. Our experimental results show that our method outperforms previous approaches, im-proving upper bounds on imbalance for 32 out of 44 instances. Table 1 summarizes our results compared to previously known bounds, highlighting the cases where we establish new record values. Our implementation draws on five different LLM models and employs various prompting strategies to generate diverse and effective streamliners, enabling a thorough exploration of the solution space.

sizes, with provably optimal solutions known only for specific dimensions. Despite advances using constraint programming, mixed integer programming, and local search methods, the computational complexity has limited progress beyond rectangles of moderate size [9, 10]. The technique of streamlining – adding constraints that focus the search on promising regions of the solution space – was initially introduced by Gomes and Sellmann [ 3 ] for related combinatorial design problems, including spatially balanced Latin squares. Streamlining constraints partition the search space, guiding the solver toward structured subspaces likely to contain high-quality solutions. While effective, the manual design of streamliners requires domain expertise and experimentation [7]. In this paper, we push the boundaries of balanced Latin rectangles by combining es-tablished streamlining techniques with the novel approach of generating streamliners using

We present an integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) with streamlining techniques to find well-balanced Latin rectangles. Our approach combines LLM-generated streamlining constraints that effectively partition the search space, directing constraint solvers toward structured subspaces containing high-quality solutions. Our methodology extends LLM-generated streamliners, as Voboril et al. (2024) introduced for decision problems, to the optimization context through techniques that incrementally refine the objective function value. We propose two complementary strategies to orchestrate sets of streamliners: an incremental mechanism that utilizes improving solutions to initialize subsequent search processes, and an evolutionary framework that maintains and refines effective streamliner populations. Our experiments demonstrate that our approach successfully reduces established minimum imbalance values for partially spatially balanced Latin rectangles across multiple problem dimensions. The results validate the efficacy of combining LLMs with constraint programming methodologies for tackling problems characterized by complex global constraints.

title: Enabling small language models to solve complex reasoning tasks
abstract: Abstract While test-time reasoning enables language models (LMs) to tackle com- plex tasks, searching or planning in natural language can be slow, costly, and error-prone. But even when LMs struggle to emulate the precise rea- soning steps needed to solve a problem, they often excel at describing its abstract structure—both how to verify solutions and how to search for them. This paper introduces DISCIPL, a method for “self-steering” LMs where a Planner model generates a task-specific inference program that is executed by a population of Follower models. Our approach equips LMs with the abil- ity to write recursive search procedures that guide LM inference, enabling new forms of verifiable and efficient reasoning. When instantiated with a small Follower (e.g., Llama-3.2-1B or Qwen3-1.7B), DISCIPL matches (and sometimes outperforms) much larger models, including GPT-4o and o1, on challenging constrained generation tasks. Our work opens up a design space of highly-parallelized Monte Carlo inference strategies that outperform standard best-of-N sampling, require no finetuning, and can be implemented automatically by existing LMs.
introduction:
Introduction

Even as language models (LMs) are becoming increasingly proficient at reasoning tasks, progress has been “jagged” (Karpathy, 2024; Roose, 2025): today’s frontier models surpass experts at science and math reasoning (e.g., Hendrycks et al., 2021; Rein et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024b) yet still routinely struggle with counting, arithmetic, tic-tac-toe, metered po- etry, and other intuitively simple tasks (Ball et al., 2024; McCoy et al., 2023; Xu & Ma, 2024). For example, even very capable LMs have difficulty writing a coherent sentence under the constraints in Fig. 1, which are manageable for most proficient English speakers.

There is a growing consensus that many problems require a more deliberate, effortful style of thinking (Kahneman, 2011). However, an open question is how to structure inference so as to best leverage available computational resources. One popular approach performs in-context reasoning via serialized chain-of-thought (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025; OpenAI, 2024b). While highly flexible, reasoning via autoregressive generation is costly, slow, and can still produce unreliable outputs. On the other hand, structured inference methods like tree search (Silver et al., 2016; Yao et al., 2023) and sequential Monte Carlo (Lew et al., 2023; Loula et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2024) attain better parallelism and efficiency by coordinating test-time computation via external algorithms. However, these methods require significant hand-engineering and rely on pre-defined scorers or verifiers, limiting their applicability.

In this work, we propose a new meta-reasoning framework called DISCIPL in which lan- guage models themselves drive the decisions for how to structure inference-time computa- tion. In our approach, a Planner LM generates an ad-hoc problem specification (encoding its understanding of the task requirements) and inference procedure (encoding its plan for how to solve the task). Importantly, the specification and plan are implemented as inference programs that invoke Follower LMs, either generatively or as likelihood evaluators. By decomposing reasoning into planning and execution, our architecture preserves flexibility while enabling orchestration of highly efficient, parallel search patterns.

Figure 1: Self-steering language models with probabilistic programs. LMs struggle with problems that combine generation and reasoning under constraints (top left, task from Yao et al., 2024). Popular approaches (top right) scale inference by search or sampling; however, even costly reasoning models do not always yield correct or fluent results (CoT example from o1, single-shot, first attempt). In our method (DISCIPL, bottom), a Planner LM writes an inference program that defines step-by-step com- putations to steer a population of Follower LMs. Our approach combines the benefits of serial and parallel methods: the Planner ensures correctness by construction, while the Followers collectively search for sequences with high probability. (See Fig. 7 for a detailed visualization.)

To test this approach, we evaluate DISCIPL on two domains: (1) COLLIE (Yao et al., 2024), a challenging constrained generation benchmark on which even very large LMs perform unreliably; and (2) PUZZLES, a custom dataset of difficult tasks involving poetry compo- sition, grant-writing, budgeting, and itinerary planning. We instantiate DISCIPL using a capable Planner LM to generate code (GPT-4o prompted with few-shot examples), and a small Follower LM to execute it (lightweight versions of Llama-3 or Qwen3). On para- graph tasks, this approach boosts accuracy well beyond the original capabilities of the Follower. Meanwhile, on more tightly constrained sentence and puzzle tasks, DISCIPL enables the Follower to surpass the Planner and approach the performance of powerful reasoning models like o1. Finally, we study how DISCIPL scales with the size and capabil- ities of the underlying Follower model, allowing our approach to benefit organically from advances in LM architecture and training methods.

Amidst a growing body of task-specific search and sampling methods for LMs (§2), we introduce a generalized approach. By using LMs to implement their own inference pro- cedures on-the-fly, DISCIPL yields efficient solutions to problems that would otherwise require non-trivial human engineering. We believe that this new combination of code gen- eration and probabilistic inference represents an exciting step in test-time scaling.

Headline:  
DISCIPL: Self-Steering Language Models Reshape Inference with Automated, Programmatic Reasoning for Constrained Generation

News Article:  

In a decisive move away from the limitations of manual engineering and model finetuning, researchers at MIT and Yale have introduced DISCIPL, a framework that enables language models (LMs) to autonomously write and execute their own inference programs—a shift that substantially enhances efficiency and accuracy on complex reasoning and constrained generation tasks ([Citation](https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.07081); [Real-World Anchor Event](https://www.marktechpost.com/2025/04/16/mit-researchers-introduce-discipl-a-self-steering-framework-using-planner-and-follower-language-models-for-efficient-constrained-generation-and-reasoning/)). By letting models themselves generate problem-specific inference procedures and orchestrate their execution, DISCIPL marks a technical departure from current approaches that depend heavily on serialized chain-of-thought mechanisms or best-of-N sampling, both of which are computationally intensive and prone to inconsistency.

At the core of DISCIPL's architectural innovation is its programmatic decomposition of inference via two distinct LM roles: the Planner and the Follower. The Planner model, typically a more capable LM, generates an explicit code-based specification of the problem—detailing both the task’s constraints and the necessary procedure for exploring candidate solutions ([Abstract]; [Introduction]). These specifications are implemented as probabilistic programs in LLAMPPL, a Python-derived framework expressly designed for leveraging language models within probabilistic programming constructs ([Real-World Anchor Event]). Once formulated, these inference programs are not executed serially; rather, a population of smaller Follower LMs is tasked with running the Planner's code in parallel. Each Follower explores the solution space by proposing and scoring partial candidates according to task-defined constraints, using techniques such as importance sampling, sequential Monte Carlo (SMC), and rejection sampling ([Real-World Anchor Event]; [Abstract]).

This division of labor and exclusive use of code generation for specifying test-time inference represent a notable advancement in how language models marshal their own reasoning resources. Instead of relying on fixed, hand-tuned search strategies or external verifiers, DISCIPL effectively leverages the Planner’s abstract problem analysis to guide Follower execution, dynamically reallocating computational bandwidth to more promising possibilities throughout inference ([Abstract]; [Introduction]). This structure directly enables *verifiable* and *efficient* reasoning, significantly reducing the error-prone and costly search typically associated with traditional language model deployments.

Empirically, DISCIPL’s operational paradigm yields performance that is competitive with, and sometimes surpasses, much larger models, even when instantiated using relatively lightweight Followers (e.g., Llama-3.2-1B, Qwen3-1.7B), on challenging constrained generation benchmarks such as COLLIE and custom datasets involving tasks like poetry composition, grant writing, and itinerary planning ([Abstract]; [Introduction]). On paragraph-level tasks, accuracy is boosted well beyond the baseline capabilities of the Follower. For tightly constrained sentence-level and puzzle tasks, DISCIPL not only enables the Follower to approach—but in some cases exceed—the Planner’s original performance as well as that of models like o1 ([Abstract]).

A key implication of DISCIPL’s architecture is its inherent scalability and adaptability. Parallel execution of Followers allows inference to scale orthogonally to generation length—a critical distinction from conventional chain-of-thought techniques, which demand extensive serial computation and exhibit escalating inference costs with longer reasoning chains ([Introduction]; [Real-World Anchor Event]). Moreover, the system requires no domain-specific finetuning or bespoke engineering; the Planner’s ability to synthesize suitable inference programs in LLAMPPL renders DISCIPL agnostic to both task structure and model architecture advancements ([Abstract]). As further improvements in LM base models emerge, the gains can be directly inherited by DISCIPL implementations.

Directly contrasting with prior test-time inference methods that either serialize reasoning (chain-of-thought) or depend on guess-and-check paradigms requiring external constraint validation, DISCIPL’s architecture generalizes beyond task-specific heuristics. By allowing language models to self-determine and instantiate their inference logic, the method not only recovers but frequently transcends the strengths of both serial and parallel approaches: the Planner guarantees correctness by design, while the Followers collaboratively search for high-probability solutions with optimized resource allocation ([Introduction]; [Real-World Anchor Event]).

This principled blend of code generation, probabilistic inference, and parallelized execution signals a compelling new direction in language modeling research—one where models do not merely generate outputs, but strategically govern *how* inference proceeds ([Real-World Anchor Event]). The research demonstrates that automated, LM-driven orchestration can unlock substantial latent capacity in smaller models, offering a scalable and generalizable path to more responsible and performant AI systems.

Interested readers and researchers are encouraged to examine the original research for a detailed exposition of DISCIPL’s methodology, empirical benchmarks, and codebase. The implications for test-time reasoning, constrained sequence modeling, and programmatically orchestrated inference suggest a rich forthcoming area for both theoretical exploration and system-level deployment. Access the full research at [DISCIPL on arXiv](https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2504.07081).

anchor_event.summary: MIT Researchers Introduce DISCIPL: A Self-Steering Framework (Result [0]) is directly relevant to the primary research, focusing on DISCIPL and the concept of self-steering language models. It references both the core technique (a Planner model generating inference programs for Follower models) and the significant advance of enabling language models to autonomously structure their own reasoning, matching the article content closely. The source is technically significant (MIT & Yale) and is not the same primary research as the original paper, making it an excellent hook.
anchor_event.full_content:
Overall, the work introduces a fresh direction in language modeling where models generate answers and devise how they should be computed. By letting the Planner generate code that structures reasoning and Followers execute this code in parallel, the method achieves precision, adaptability, and fluency without requiring larger models or manual engineering. The research’s results illustrate a clear path for enabling smaller language models to outperform their size through intelligent orchestration and self-guided inference.

The inner workings of DISCIPL involve generating inference code using a language called LLAMPPL, which is a Python-based framework for probabilistic programming with language models. The Planner writes code that defines how to explore possible solutions, while Follower models run the code to search for valid outputs. These programs operate by iteratively proposing partial solutions and scoring them based on constraints. The architecture supports multiple inference techniques, including importance sampling, sequential Monte Carlo (SMC), and rejection sampling, which are scalable based on computational budgets. This structured decomposition lets the system reallocate resources to more promising candidates during execution, improving precision and efficiency.

Currently, methods like chain-of-thought prompting attempt to guide models through a reasoning path, but these are limited by their serial execution and expensive inference costs. Parallel approaches such as guess-and-check or best-of-N sampling rely on generating and filtering multiple candidates. Yet, they need separate scoring mechanisms and often yield inconsistent results. These tools improve performance slightly but cannot guarantee the satisfaction of all constraints, especially when models lack an inherent understanding of those constraints.

title: Resolving to spend less time on your smartphone Understanding your travel habits can help, say researchers
abstract: Habits are a fundamental driver of human behavior. However, prior research has largely studied habits in isolation. This study investigates spatial and smartphone habits—two cornerstone habits in contemporary society—in tandem, revealing the multiplicative ways in which habitual processes can underlie human behavior. We provide evidence that smartphone habits are stronger in habitually traveled and visited spaces. We leverage a custom-designed app that logged mobility and app use while prompting map-based questionnaires of participants’ trips for two weeks (N=419 participants; n=27,446,977 GPS and app logs; n=7,226 trip questionnaires). Overall, app habit strength predicted higher likelihood of app use (vs. non-use) and more app use (when used) on more habitually travelled routes and in more habitually visited destinations. The interaction between spatial and smartphone habits was most consistent for objective (vs. subjective) habit indicators. Social app habits were stronger and more context-independent than non-social app habits. Altogether, our findings reveal how distinct habitual processes can combine to shape daily behavior. Further, our method illustrates the potential of linking mobile sensing and surveying to measure habits in the real world.
introduction:
Habits are a direct and deep-rooted driver of human behavior1,2. By automating our cognition, habitual processes can make the world easier to navigate. Yet, they can also conflict with our goals3,4. For better and for worse, the degree to which behavior is performed habitually has powerful implications for how we engage with the world around us. Although people engage in a wide variety of habits in daily life, habits are typically studied in isolation5. By zooming out from this standard scope, we can reveal broader, multiplicative ways that habitual processes underlie real-world behavior across domains of everyday life.

Habitual behaviors are cued and performed automatically after being frequently rewarded in stable contexts2. In turn, the strength of a habit is indicated by the level of frequency (i.e., number of occurrences) and automaticity (i.e., lack of conscious processes)6. In contemporary society, spatial and smartphone habits act as cornerstone habits. Although a variety of factors can contribute to mobility choices and mobile phone use in a given situation, past research across disciplines indicates that habit is a leading predictor of both7,8. Critically, the interaction between these two types of habits is ideally suited for analyzing interactive habitual processes in daily life, as smartphone habits are uniquely activated across spaces9,10. Exploring their interactive relationship is also vital given the growing integration of mobility and technology: a convergence with implications for numerous facets of human behavior11–16.

Spatial habits are a prominent form of habitual behavior, as people often select and engage with their everyday spaces out of habit7,8. Scholars have extensively examined the role of habit in the selection of routes17–21, or how individuals travel from an origin to a destination via a particular mode of transportation (e.g., walking). Here, we focus on the role of habit in how people select routes as well as destinations, which together comprise the activity spaces that people inhabit in daily life22. Thus, spatial habits are comprised of places (i.e., destinations) and how individuals move between them (i.e., routes). Spatial habits can be cued by myriad factors (e.g., time, trip purpose)23. Research in cognitive geography has investigated spatial habits through the study of navigation, spatial cognition, and mental mapping. A notable example is anchor-point theory, which examines how people perceive and represent space through reference points like landmarks or neighborhoods24. Moreover, the centrality of spatial behavior in past habit research reflects the repetitive nature of daily mobility patterns identified in large-scale computational studies25,26. Spatial habits thereby have unique potential to operate as foundational habits: they determine the stable contexts that offer cues (or 'triggers') for other daily habits.

Smartphone habits are another dominant form of habitual behavior in modern society, as well as a heuristic case in habit research27. By bundling many possible behaviors (e.g., social media scrolling, podcast listening) into one handheld object, smartphones facilitate the development of a wide variety of activation cues (e.g., times, user activities), including personalized sets of cues tied to specific apps28. Accordingly, smartphone habits can be uniquely activated across a variety of spatial contexts9,10—challenging the presumed 'context-dependence' of traditional media habits and presumed role of location for cueing habits at-large2. However, some studies reveal that certain types of apps are also more likely to be used in certain types of places, as routes and destinations afford certain activities29,30. In this way, specific smartphone behaviors may develop implicit associations with specific types of spaces31,32—such as more habitually selected contexts33. In other words, spatial habits have the potential to make smartphone habits stronger.

To test this proposition, we examine whether more habitual apps are used disproportionately more in (1) habitually travelled routes and (2) habitually visited destinations. We test whether habitual apps can be interwoven with transportation habits by evaluating app use on routes. For example, app use and specific spaces may become embedded in scripts34: a habitual route (e.g., driving from home to work) can cue an app habit (e.g., listening to Spotify) at rehearsed points during the trip (e.g., traffic lights). These scripts may also be prompted by specific cues (e.g., time of day, social setting), thereby coupling spatial habits (e.g., the morning commute) and smartphone habits (e.g., listening to a news podcast) through sequences of cued actions. Additionally, we test the downstream influence of spatial habits on smartphone use by evaluating app use in destinations (e.g., the workplace). Since spaces chosen out of habit are more familiar35, habitually visited destinations may be more likely to contain familiar cues that activate app habits (e.g., checking a social media app while cleaning the kitchen). Altogether, we test whether app habits are activated more when individuals choose routes and destinations out of habit.

We further examine the robustness of the interaction between spatial and smartphone habits by exploring the distinctive role of social vs. non-social apps. Social apps provide a direct channel for social needs and norms to be activated habitually in daily life36. Based on the potency of social rewards in habit formation, along with the high proportion of social app usage in studies of overall smartphone usage37,38, social apps are expected to exhibit higher habit strength27,39. The theorized strength of social app habits may derive from their reliance on internal, non-spatial cues40. For example, people may habitually check social apps throughout the day in response to or in anticipation of new messages27,37. Thus, social app habits may be more likely to be context-independent: triggered across a variety of spatial contexts39.

Habits present a unique measurement challenge because they are triggered and performed with minimal conscious awareness throughout daily life41. Traditionally, habit research has centered on establishing the causal role of habit versus more conscious processes such as goals27. Here, moving beyond the question of whether habits or goals matter more, we focus on the real-world conditions—or contexts—under which habits become more likely to be activated. Accordingly, the current study sought to capture habits in the real world. By coupling GPS and app logs with map-based questionnaires, we collected objective and subjective indicators of spatial and smartphone habits and used those indicators to predict app use in context.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Habitual Commutes Reinforce Smartphone App Use: Empirical Evidence Unveils Multiplicative Interplay Between Mobility and Digital Behavioral Patterns

News Article:  
A comprehensive empirical study leveraging 27,446,977 GPS and app usage logs from 419 participants reveals a robust connection between habitual commuting routines and reinforced smartphone habit strength within familiar environments. Specifically, the likelihood of app use—and the intensity of engagement with those apps—escalates on habitually travelled routes and at destinations visited out of habit. By instrumenting a custom-designed smartphone application, researchers systematically linked individual mobility patterns with objective and subjective indicators of digital behavior, ultimately providing compelling evidence that spatial and smartphone habits interact multiplicatively to shape daily behavioral scripts.

This research addresses a crucial gap in computational behavioral science: moving beyond the traditional examination of habits in isolation, it rigorously quantifies how daily mobility (spatial habits) and digital consumption (smartphone habits) couple to produce routine behaviors. The study foregrounds the foundational role of spatial habits, positing that habitually selected places and routes act not only as stable contexts (or 'triggers') but also as cues that potentiate entrenched smartphone routines. For instance, a consistently traversed route—such as a daily commute—may serve as the behavioral scaffold for recurring app engagement, effectively embedding digital behaviors within locational scripts. These findings align directly with real-world observations, as highlighted by Tech Xplore’s coverage, which describes how smartphone use intensifies during commutes through familiar, routine environments.

Methodologically, the study’s custom app logged extensive mobility and application usage data, supplemented with over 7,000 map-based trip questionnaires. The researchers decomposed habit strength into frequency and automaticity, enabling precise assessment of both spatial and smartphone habits. Crucially, robust statistical analysis established that app habit strength is a significant predictor of both the probability and the extent of app use on highly habitual routes and at destinations regularly visited out of habit. The interaction between spatial and smartphone habits was particularly pronounced for objective habit indicators, whereas subjective measures displayed more variability. Furthermore, the research delineates the distinction between social and non-social app habits: social apps exhibited greater habit strength and were markedly more context-independent, supporting the hypothesis that social interaction mechanisms activate digital behaviors across broader spatial settings.

This evidence-based interplay between spatial cues and digital engagement provides a new lens for both digital well-being interventions and technology platform design. Most notably, practical strategies to mitigate excessive smartphone use are substantiated by the study’s core findings. Given that habitual environments act as potent activators of app usage, disrupting these contextual cues—such as by establishing 'phone-free zones' within regular commutes or familiar locations—directly targets the underlying behavioral mechanisms. Tech Xplore’s summary advocates for actionable steps including designating phone-free zones, leveraging grayscale display modes, and creating physical separation from smartphones in recurring contexts. By targeting the intersection of spatial and digital routine, these interventions are poised to be significantly more effective than context-agnostic approaches.

The technical implications extend to the domain of mobility and digital platform engineering. As mobility platforms evolve—embracing multi-mobility integrations and contextualized user experiences—the empirical understanding that spatial and digital habits are mutually reinforcing provides new opportunities for optimization. Platforms can now integrate contextual habit signals, such as route familiarity and temporal patterns, to tailor app engagement strategies, personalize notification schemas, or introduce digital well-being nudges precisely at moments of heightened habitual risk. The study also provides a methodological template, illustrating the advantages of coupling high granularity mobile sensing with ecological momentary assessments, affording robust measurement of habit strength in real-world settings.

In sum, this research marks an inflection point in habit science and applied technology, empirically establishing that our physical interaction with space and our digital routines are not merely parallel behaviors, but are deeply interwoven through repeated contextual coupling. For digital health, mobility innovation, and computational social science, leveraging these findings is essential for aligned intervention and next-generation platform development.

For a deeper technical analysis of these emergent, cross-domain habitual processes and their implications, access the full research paper at: https://dx.doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-25174-2

title: AI can pick up cultural values by mimicking how kids learn
abstract:
Constructing a universal moral code for artificial intelligence (AI) is challenging because human cultures have different values, norms, and social practices. We

therefore argue that AI systems should adapt to culture based on observation: Just

as a child raised in a particular culture learns the specific values, norms, and behav-
introduction:
1 Introduction

A formidable challenge in the field of artificial intelligence (AI) is endowing AI agents with the richness and depth of values and behaviors that characterize human cultures and their decision-making tendencies. State-of-the-art AI systems today [1–3] are based on large-language models (LLMs), which are neural networks with billions of parameters trained on data harvested from across the internet, leading

Data availability statement: Code and data for replicating the findings in this article are available at: https:// github.com/JasmineLi-805/moral-ai-irl.

to a “one-size-fits-all” (rather than pluralistic) AI system [4,5] (see also [6,7]). In this article, we propose that rather than attempting to handcraft or learn a universal code of values and behaviors for AI, AI agents should implicitly learn them by being embedded in the human culture in which they are operating [8]. We use the term “culture” here to refer to the dynamic values, norms, and behaviors shared by a social group [9], acknowledging that it is a dynamic phenomenon—a cultural cycle in which humans continuously shape and adopt values and behaviors [10,11]. The approach we propose would endow AI agents with the ability to deal with such cul- tural dynamics in a manner that is tailored to the culture or social group in which the AI agent was “raised.” This recognizes the fact that what is acceptable or norma- tive in one culture may not be acceptable in another [12], necessitating AI systems that are capable of adapting to the values and behavioral tendencies of the culture in which they have been deployed. This will be challenging for AI and some intellec- tual humility is demanded, in as much as even humans, the creators of the AI, have a hard time appreciating and accepting the values and behaviors of cultures different from their own [12,13]. Might future AI surpass humans in such flexibility?

Funding: This work was supported by the Templeton World Charity Foundation (RPNR, ANM), the National Science Foundation EFRI Grant no. 2223495 (RPNR) and Grant no. 2230466 (KR, ANM), a UW + Amazon Science Hub grant (RPNR), a grant from the Bezos Family Foundation (ANM), and the Cherng Jia & Elizabeth Yun Hwang Professorship (RPNR). https://www.templetonworldcharity.org/ https://www.nsf.gov/ https://www.sciencehub.uw.edu/ https://www.bezosfamilyfoundation.org/ The funders did not play any role in the study design, data collection and analysis, decision to publish, or preparation of the manuscript.

Competing interests: The authors have declared that no competing interests exist.

1.1 Altruism as a cultural value for AI to learn

In this article, we focus on the human tendency to act in a way that benefits others, even if it comes at a cost to oneself, often labeled “altruistic behavior.” For example, a person might give precious resources (such as food, money, or time) to someone else without directly benefiting themselves. In cases of extreme altruism, these acts can be directed towards strangers even in situations when the actor has no expectancy for reciprocal action or reward. There are significant between-country differences in altruistic tendencies across industrialized nations [14], small-scale traditional cultures [15], and even in children as a function of cultural background [16], motivating the need to investigate whether an AI system could learn altruistic behaviors from observation.

Our approach draws on evidence about how neural mechanisms in the brain support normative social behavior: the same reward systems thought to be involved in reinforcement-based learning are also harnessed to learn the dominant norms and values within a society [17–19]. The resulting system governs human cultural expectations and ways of interacting with others [20,21]. To learn what the accepted behavior is in any given context, the brain must first develop expectation rules about events and learn internal “value systems,” including the benefits of certain behav- iors as well as the costs of deviating from the norm. For example, by perceiving or producing altruistic behaviors within a culture—such as giving others items of value or otherwise acting to benefit others even at a cost to oneself—a child or an ado- lescent may learn to connect, in a certain context, a “positive” value with behaving altruistically [16,22].

There has been considerable AI research on using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) and imitation learning to mimic human actions [23–29]. More recently, IRL has been used to learn human value systems in specific contexts such as route choice modeling [30], and multi-objective IRL/RL methods have been proposed for

human value alignment [31–34]. Here, we use IRL to address the question: Can an AI agent learn cultural values and behavioral tendencies for specific social scenarios by observing the behavior of humans from different cultural groups, and then generalize this learning to a new context?

1.2 Using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to train AI on human culture

IRL is a technique developed in the field of machine learning [23,26,28] to enable an AI agent to learn a new skill from human demonstrations; rather than simply mimicking the exact actions of the human, IRL attempts to learn a “reward function” from the human demonstrations. The reward function assigns positive or negative values (“rewards” or “penal- ties”) to particular features of the observed demonstration. By learning an appropriate reward function that is aligned with the presumed reward function being used by the human in the demonstrations, the AI agent is able to imitate the human behavior and generalize beyond simple mimicry of human actions.

In this article, we show how IRL can be used to train AI agents that can infer and learn the reward functions of humans belonging to a particular cultural group. The learned reward function assigns specific reward values (positive or negative) to situations in which the AI agent may find itself. By choosing actions that optimize total expected reward based on its learned reward function, the AI agent can behave in a manner that is attuned to the human culture in which it is embed- ded. A major advantage of this approach is that the agent can assign reward values to scenarios that are related to but not exactly the same as previously encountered scenarios. This allows the agent to generalize its decision-making ability, including those involving societal values and behavioral tendencies, to novel situations, emulating the first- and second- order generalization children demonstrate based on cultural learning and experience [16,35,36].

To test this approach, we collected data from human participants playing an online game involving simple forms of altruism and fairness, both of which have been regarded as important components of human morality and culture [37–42]. We recruited participants with cultural backgrounds associated with different altruistic behaviors [43], specifically, US participants who identified as White or as Latino (we use the term “Latino” to denote anyone born in or with ancestors from Latin America, regardless of gender).

Our hypothesis regarding differences in altruistic behavior is motivated by prior work showing that, within the same nation-state (here, the US), Latinos tend to be more collectivistic [44,45] and tend to have a more interdependent self- concept than Whites, who are generally characterized as being more individualistic and prone to seeing themselves as independent of others [46–48]. Whereas individualistic cultures tend to emphasize personal achievement and autonomy, collectivist cultures often prioritize group harmony and the achievements and welfare of others over personal gains. We hypothesize that, within the novel context of our online “world,” Latinos will exhibit more altruistic behavior than Whites. That is, subgroups of people within the US share cultural tendencies that they may bring to an online game, which we hypothesize can be inferred through IRL by an AI agent.

Note that rather than using data from human adults who make explicit value judgments, as in the case of the Moral Machines experiment involving the ethics of self-driving cars [49] or the trolley problem [50,51] (see also [52]), or data from descriptive judgments of scenarios presenting values and norms, as in the Delphi experiment [53] (see also [4,5]), our approach seeks to emulate the way a human child absorbs values and norms implicitly from observation and inter- actions with people in their culture [16,54]. Our approach shares similarities with previous work in reinforcement learn- ing [55,56] and brain-inspired affective learning [57] showing how an AI agent could learn ethical characteristics such as altruism, empathy, and sympathy; these approaches did not train their AI agent on human data (as in our study here) and did not focus on cultural differences. Finally, our online game scenario includes an element of fairness, in addition to altruism: the “privileged” player has to endure some costs to help the other player who has been placed in an unfair and potentially less rewarding circumstance. We hypothesize that the reward functions inferred by IRL from human data may implicitly capture this aspect of the game scenario.

To summarize, this article tests the following hypotheses:

H1: Cultural background and exposure to altruistic behavior may influence participants’ tendencies to display altruistic

behavior.

H2: An AI agent using inverse reinforcement learning can learn culturally attuned, quantifiable altruistic behaviors and

values for specific social scenarios by observing the behavior of humans from different cultural groups.

H3: An AI agent can generalize learned social values and behavior to a new context.

The remainder of the article focuses on the above hypothesis and presents our results. We first describe our experimen- tal studies with humans and present results showing that an AI agent can learn from observing the behavior of a particular cultural group (Latinos or Whites) an IRL reward function capturing the altruistic tendencies of that group. We then show how this learning can generalize to new problems involving other altruistic decision making. Taken together, these results, though obtained from a simple online game scenario, point the way towards future AI agents that could become attuned to the culture in which they are operating by continually learning and adjusting their behavior from observing and interacting with humans within that culture.

**Headline:**  
University of Washington Researchers Probe AI’s Capacity for Learning Culturally-Specific Altruism

**News Article:**

Researchers at the University of Washington (UW) are at the forefront of examining whether artificial intelligence (AI) can be trained not only to act altruistically but also to adapt its behavior in accordance with local cultural norms governing moral behaviors such as altruism and fairness. Central to this investigation is the proposition that AI should not be governed by static, universal ethical codes, but rather should dynamically learn and internalize the values of the human culture in which it is operating. The UW study operationalizes this concept by leveraging Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) to enable AI agents to infer and adopt contextually grounded reward functions, thereby capturing the nuanced moral and behavioral tendencies of observed cultural groups ([Citation](https://dx.doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0337914); [Real-World Anchor Event]).

The project’s technical core centers on the systemic limitations of current AI models, particularly large language models (LLMs), which derive their training data primarily from North America and Europe ([Introduction]). As articulated by Meltzoff, the preponderance of Western-centric data in existing AI systems risks producing normative outputs that may diverge significantly from the values and expectations held by end-users from other cultural backgrounds ([Real-World Anchor Event]). This challenge is amplified by the heterogeneous and evolving nature of human cultures, whose values, norms, and practices are not static but are dynamically co-constructed and continually adapted within social groups ([Abstract], [Introduction]).

To interrogate whether AI systems can internalize pluralistic moral values, the UW researchers constructed four distinct datasets: altruistic, non-altruistic, Latino American, and white, non-Latino American. These datasets were derived from an online game scenario designed to elicit fundamental social behaviors—specifically, basic forms of altruism and fairness ([Introduction], [Real-World Anchor Event]). The selection of these datasets not only allows for the disambiguation of culture-specific altruistic behaviors but also provides granular empirical input for the IRL training pipeline. In this context, IRL is deployed not simply to enable imitation but to facilitate the learning of latent reward functions that underlie observed human actions ([Introduction], [1.2 Using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to train AI on human culture]).

IRL serves as the methodological linchpin in this study. Unlike direct imitation learning, which constrains agents to mimic explicit actions, IRL enables AI systems to infer a reward structure from observed demonstrations, ascribing specific positive or negative values to defined features of behavior ([1.2 Using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to train AI on human culture]). This paradigm allows AI to generalize learned behaviors to novel contexts, echoing the way children abstract and extend cultural norms from observed interactions within their social milieu ([Abstract], [Introduction]). The UW group’s approach thus aligns training protocols for AI with neurocognitive evidence of how human brains utilize reinforcement-based signaling to learn both individual and group-level normative behaviors ([Introduction]).

The choice to focus on dynamic cultural adaptation is technically significant because it counters the “one-size-fits-all” approach prevalent in current LLM architectures ([Abstract]). Rather than encoding a fixed moral framework, the proposed IRL-driven approach permits AI agents to adjust their decision-making by observing and internalizing the behavioral tendencies prevalent among demonstrators belonging to specific cultural cohorts ([Abstract], [Introduction]). The study specifically considers intra-national differences—using Latino and White participants resident in the US—to test whether an AI system can distinguish between, learn from, and ultimately replicate culturally contingent expressions of altruism.

In operational terms, scenarios were constructed such that a “privileged” player could incur a cost to assist another—thus embedding both fairness and altruistic incentives in gameplay ([1.2 Using inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to train AI on human culture]). The resultant behavior datasets were then used to train AI agents via IRL, in order to produce reward functions sensitive to the behavioral patterns exhibited by each cultural group. The hypothesis underpinning this design—motivated by prior work on collectivist vs. individualist tendencies ([Introduction])—is that AI agents exposed to Latino participants would infer more collectivistic, altruistic reward functions, while those trained on White participant data would reflect more individualistic tendencies.

The principal technical contributions, validated in this controlled scenario, are threefold:  
1. Demonstration that cultural background shapes observable altruistic behaviors in humans.  
2. Empirical evidence that IRL enables AI agents to learn quantitative, culturally specific reward functions from behavioral observation.  
3. Indication that such AI agents can generalize learned cultural values to novel scenarios, beyond rote repetition ([Abstract], [Introduction], [Citation]).

This work signals a pivotal step toward constructing AI systems capable of adaptive, pluralistic moral and social reasoning, responsive to the lived realities and normative expectations of local user populations. It suggests directions for future research on deploying IRL-based agents in more complex and dynamic sociocultural environments and underscores the superiority of embedded learning over attempting to handcraft or universally encode moral rules.

For expert readers seeking a rigorous examination of culturally adaptive AI and the technical mechanisms enabling value alignment, the full dataset, codebase, and detailed methodological exposition are openly accessible at [GitHub](https://github.com/JasmineLi-805/moral-ai-irl). Access the original research paper for comprehensive results and theoretical implications: [PLoS ONE: Constructing AI that learns local moral values from cultural observation](https://dx.doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0337914).
