title: Researchers reveal bias in a widely used measure of algorithm performance
abstract:
Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection

Normalized mutual information is widely used as a similarity measure for evaluating the In this paper, we argue that results performance of clustering and classification algorithms. returned by the normalized mutual information are biased for two reasons: first, because they ignore the information content of the contingency table and, second, because their symmetric normalization introduces spurious dependence on algorithm output. We introduce a modified version of the mutual information that remedies both of these shortcomings. As a practical demonstration of the importance of using an unbiased measure, we perform extensive numer- ical tests on a basket of popular algorithms for network community detection and show that one’s conclusions about which algorithm is best are significantly affected by the biases in the traditional mutual information.
introduction:
1 Introduction

A common task in data analysis is the comparison of two different labelings of a set of objects. How well do demographics predict political affiliation? How accurately do blood tests predict clinical outcomes? These are questions about how similar one labeling of individuals/items/results is to another and they are commonly tackled using the information theoretic measure known as mutual information, in which an experimental or computational measure of some kind is compared against a ground truth.

Mutual information works by asking of how efficiently we can describe one labeling if we already know the other [1]. Specifically, it measures how much less information it takes to communicate the first labeling if we know the second versus if we do not. As an example, mutual information is commonly used in network science to evaluate the performance of algorithms for network commu- nity detection [2]. One takes a network whose community structure is already known and applies a community detection algorithm to it to infer the communities. Then one uses mutual information to compare the output of the algorithm to the known correct communities. Algorithms that con-

sistently achieve high mutual information scores are considered good. We will use this application as an illustrative example later in the paper.

Mutual information has a number of appealing properties as a tool for comparing labelings. It is invariant under permutations of the labels, so that, for example, two divisions of a network into communities that differ only in the numbering of the communities will be correctly identified as being the same. It also returns sensible results in cases where the number of distinct label values is not the same in the two labelings. However, the standard mutual information measure also has some significant shortcomings, and two in particular that we highlight in this paper. First, it has a bias towards labelings with too many distinct label values. For instance, a community detection algorithm that incorrectly divides networks into significantly more groups than are present in the ground truth can nonetheless achieve a high mutual information score. A number of approaches for correcting this flaw have been proposed. One can apply direct penalties for incorrect numbers of groups [3] or subtract correction terms based on the average mutual information over some ensemble of candidate labelings [4, 5] or on the statistics of the contingency table [6, 7]. For reasons we discuss shortly, we favor the latter approach, which leads to the measure known as the reduced mutual information.

The second drawback of the mutual information arises when the measure is normalized, as it commonly is to improve interpretability. The most popular normalization scheme creates a measure that runs between zero and one by dividing the mutual information by the arithmetic mean of the entropies of the two labelings being compared [8], although one can also normalize by the minimum, maximum, or geometric mean of the entropies. As we demonstrate in this paper, however, these normalizations introduce biases into the results by comparison with the unnormalized measure, because the normalization factor depends on the candidate labeling as well as the ground truth. This effect can be large enough to change scientific outcomes, and we provide examples of this phenomenon.

In order to avoid this latter bias, while still retaining the interpretability of a normalized mutual information measure, we favor normalizing by the entropy of the ground-truth labeling alone. This removes the source of bias but introduces an asymmetry in the normalization. At first sight this asymmetry may seem undesirable, and previous authors have gone to some lengths to avoid it. Here, however, we argue that it is not only justified but actually desirable, for several reasons. First, many of the classification problems we consider are unaffected by the asymmetry, since they involve the comparison of one or more candidate labelings against a single, unchanging ground truth. Moreover, by contrast with the multitude of possible symmetric normalizations, the asymmetric measure we propose is the unique way to normalize the mutual information without introducing biases due to the normalization itself.

More broadly, the asymmetric measure is more informative than the conventional symmetric one. Consider for instance the common situation where the groups or communities in one labeling are a subdivision of those in the other. We might for example label individuals by the country they live in on the one hand and by the town or city on the other. Then the more detailed labeling tells us everything there is to know about the coarser one, but the reverse is not true. Telling you the city fixes the country, but not vice versa. Thus one could argue that the mutual information between the two eshould be asymmetric, and this type of asymmetry will be a key feature of the measures we study.

Both drawbacks of the standard mutual information—bias towards too many groups and depen- dence of the normalization on the candidate labeling—can be addressed simultaneously by using an asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information as defined in this paper. In support of this approach we present an extensive comparison of the performance of this and other variants of the mutual information in network community detection tasks, generating a large number of random

test networks with known community structure and a variety of structural parameters, and then attempting to recover the communities using popular community detection algorithms. Within this framework, we find that conclusions about which algorithms perform best are significantly impacted by the choice of mutual information measure, and specifically that traditional measures erroneously favor algorithms that find too many communities, but our proposed measure does not. Code im- plementing our measure can be found at https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
New Study Reveals Critical Bias in Normalized Mutual Information, Proposes Corrective Asymmetric Measure

News Article:  
In a paper published in _Nature Communications_ ([Jerdee, Kirkley, Newman, 2025](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8)), researchers Max Jerdee (Santa Fe Institute), Alec Kirkley (University of Hong Kong), and Mark Newman (University of Michigan) provide compelling evidence that the normalized mutual information (NMI)—a ubiquitous metric in machine learning, network science, and information theory—is fundamentally biased. Their findings highlight not only significant technical flaws in widely adopted similarity measures, but also the profound impact these flaws can exert on empirical conclusions in classification and community detection studies. As Newman notes, despite thousands of papers referencing or relying on NMI, the metric’s inherent bias is “large enough to change scientific conclusions in some cases.” This revelation calls for the urgent reassessment of algorithm evaluation protocols across research domains that utilize NMI as a standard.

### Technical Critique of NMI: Unveiling the Mechanisms of Bias

Central to many data analysis tasks is the comparison of two different labelings—such as predicted versus ground-truth classifications in supervised learning, or detected versus known communities in network analysis. Mutual information (MI) is traditionally employed to quantify the shared information between such labelings, offering properties such as invariance under label permutations and robustness to label cardinality mismatch. However, common practice normalizes MI—typically by the arithmetic mean of the labeling entropies—producing the normalized mutual information (NMI) score for interpretable, unit-bounded output.

Jerdee, Kirkley, and Newman’s work rigorously demonstrates two interconnected technical flaws in NMI when used as an evaluation measure for clustering and classification:

1. **Information Content Ignorance of the Contingency Table:** The conventional NMI measure neglects nuanced information embedded within the contingency table of labelings. This oversight enables clustering algorithms that artificially inflate the number of detected groups to achieve undeservedly high similarity scores, even when the underlying partition is incorrect.
   
2. **Bias Introduced by Symmetric Normalization:** The prevailing NMI normalization schemes—whether by arithmetic, minimum, or geometric mean of labeling entropies—introduce spurious dependence on the candidate labeling. Rather than exclusively reflecting correspondence with ground truth, the NMI becomes sensitive to the structure of the algorithm’s output, independent of its substantive agreement with the reference. As verified in their experimental framework, this normalization factor is not invariant, inducing systematic biases that can actively reorder the perceived performance hierarchy of algorithms.

Notably, [external reporting](https://www.santafe.edu/news-center/news/researchers-reveal-bias-in-a-widely-used-measure-of-algorithm-performance) corroborates that these biases enable algorithms which overpartition data (i.e., produce too many communities or classes) to appear unduly favorable, potentially distorting the landscape of published benchmarks and comparative algorithmic studies.

### Empirical Evidence and Redefining Algorithm Evaluation

The authors substantiate these theoretical critiques through extensive numerical testing over a diverse array of network community detection algorithms. By generating synthetic networks with known ground-truth community structure and systematically varying structural parameters, they directly observe that traditional NMI scores erroneously prioritize algorithms that return excessive numbers of clusters. This misrepresentation is not a mere artifact but persists across algorithm classes and network typologies.

For instance, the paper details scenarios where applying standard NMI decisively, but incorrectly, favors algorithms whose outputs bear little semantic connection to the ground truth, solely because they deliver partitions with high group counts. In contrast, the discrepancy vanishes with the application of a more rigorous metric: the asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information.

### The Case for Asymmetric Normalization: Technical Rationale and Practical Implications

To resolve these intertwined deficiencies, Jerdee, Kirkley, and Newman introduce an asymmetric normalization scheme. Specifically, they advocate for normalizing the reduced mutual information (a statistic that corrects the base MI for random agreement as quantified by the statistics of the contingency table) exclusively by the entropy of the ground-truth labeling. This construction is intentionally asymmetric: it eschews the “fairness” of symmetric normalization for a mathematically grounded focus on correct reference comparison. The rationale is decisive—many classification problems in machine learning and network analysis are unidirectional, evaluating variable or candidate partitions against a fixed gold standard. In such cases, symmetric normalization is neither methodologically justified nor empirically necessary. Moreover, the asymmetric variant is the unique normalization strategy that avoids introducing further bias through dependency on the candidate labeling.

This asymmetric perspective proves not only theoretically robust but also empirically superior. The authors show that conclusions regarding the “best” algorithm for community detection change markedly when switching from conventional NMI to the asymmetric normalized reduced MI. The corrected measure mitigates the well-documented tendency for NMI to reward spurious overpartitioning, enabling algorithmic performance comparisons that genuinely reflect substantive predictive or structural correspondence.

### Consequences for Research Practice and Future Directions

By systematically exposing and addressing both sources of NMI bias, this work has immediate ramifications for the empirical evaluation of clustering, classification, and community detection algorithms. Research domains relying on benchmark comparisons—ranging from computational biology to social network analysis—are advised to transition to the provided corrective metric to ensure the rigor and reproducibility of scientific conclusions.

For those wishing to incorporate this unbiased measure into their experimental workflows, the authors have released implementation code at https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi.

This study compels the expert community to re-examine evaluation protocols and calls for the adoption of the asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information for future work. Read the full research for an in-depth technical development and empirical demonstration: [“Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection” (_Nature Communications_, December 11, 2025)](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8).

**Read the paper “Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection” in _Nature Communications_ (December 11, 2025). DOI: [10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66150-8)**

In a paper published in Nature Communications, SFI Postdoctoral Fellow [Max Jerdee](https://www.santafe.edu/people/profile/max-jerdee), Alec Kirkley (University of Hong Kong), and SFI External Professor [Mark Newman](https://www.santafe.edu/people/profile/mark-newman) (University of Michigan) show that NMI — one of the most widely used metrics in data science and network research — can produce biased results. "Normalized mutual information has been used or referenced in thousands of papers in the decades since it was first proposed,” Newman says, “but it turns out that it can give incorrect results, and the errors are large enough to change scientific conclusions in some cases."

When scientists test algorithms that sort or classify data, they often turn to a trusted tool called Normalized Mutual Information (or NMI) to measure how well an algorithm’s output matches reality. But according to new research, that tool may not be as reliable as many assume.

However, it partially fails the BRIDGE CONSTRAINT as it does not explicitly connect the technical critique of NMI biases to the specific findings of Jerdee, Kirkley, and Newman in a detailed and coherent manner. The draft provides a broad overview but lacks explicit, focused bridging of the technical critique to the specific findings and practical implications of their work.

The draft effectively introduces the new research paper and highlights the biases in normalized mutual information (NMI). However, it fails to sufficiently bridge the technical critique of NMI biases to the specific findings of Jerdee, Kirkley, and Newman. While the draft mentions overemphasis on overpartitioning and symmetric normalization, it does not explicitly connect these biases to the incorrect scientific conclusions detailed in their work. Additionally, the draft does not articulate clearly why the corrected, asymmetrically normalized measure is essential for accurate algorithm evaluation and how it addresses the identified biases.

The draft partially satisfies the target rubrics but lacks explicit bridging between the technical critique of NMI biases and the specific findings of Jerdee, Kirkley, and Newman. While it mentions their work and the biases in NMI, it does not sufficiently detail how reliance on NMI leads to incorrect scientific conclusions with concrete examples from their research. Additionally, the explanation of why the corrected, asymmetrically normalized measure is essential lacks depth, particularly in how it addresses the identified biases and its practical implications for fields like machine learning and network analysis.

The draft effectively introduces the biases in normalized mutual information (NMI) and highlights the need for a corrected measure, but it falls short in explicitly bridging the technical critique of NMI biases to the specific findings of Jerdee, Kirkley, and Newman. The draft does not provide concrete examples from their work, nor does it explicitly detail how reliance on NMI leads to incorrect scientific conclusions. Additionally, the explanation of why the corrected, asymmetrically normalized measure is essential lacks depth and does not fully address the identified biases or emphasize the practical implications for fields like machine learning and network analysis.

title: New method improves the reliability of statistical estimations
abstract: Estimating associations between spatial covariates and responses — rather than merely predicting responses — is central to environmental science, epidemiology, and economics. For instance, public health officials might be interested in whether air pollution has a strictly positive association with a health outcome, and the mag- nitude of any effect. Standard machine learning methods often provide accurate predictions but offer limited insight into covariate-response relationships. And we show that existing methods for constructing confidence (or credible) intervals for associations can fail to provide nominal coverage in the face of model misspecifi- cation and nonrandom locations — despite both being essentially always present in spatial problems. We introduce a method that constructs valid frequentist confi- dence intervals for associations in spatial settings. Our method requires minimal assumptions beyond a form of spatial smoothness and a homoskedastic Gaussian error assumption. In particular, we do not require model correctness or covariate overlap between training and target locations. Our approach is the first to guarantee nominal coverage in this setting and outperforms existing techniques in both real and simulated experiments. Our confidence intervals are valid in finite samples when the noise of the Gaussian error is known, and we provide an asymptotically consistent estimation procedure for this noise variance when it is unknown.
introduction:
Introduction

Scientists and social scientists often seek to understand the direction and magnitude of associations in settings where variables vary spatially. And since these associations are often used to inform policy, communicating uncertainties is crucial. Example associations of interest include the relationship between aerosol concentrations and regional precipitation changes [76], the link between proximity to major highways and the prevalence of dementia [35], and the link between air pollution exposure and birth weight [34]. In each case, the covariates (e.g., aerosol concentrations, proximity to highways, and air pollution) may be viewed as functions of spatial location. Moreover, scientists often have data at some spatial locations but want to infer associations at others. For instance, a country might measure birth weight and air pollution at the municipal level for some municipalities and wish to understand their relationship in municipalities without data.

Our goal in the present work is to provide valid and useful confidence intervals for an estimator of these associations when (i) the underlying model may be misspecified and (ii) inference is needed at spatial locations that may differ from those in the observed data. First we argue that existing methods do not already solve this problem. In particular, we make this argument in turn for modern flexible machine learning methods, linear methods, spatial regression methods, and debiasing approaches.

∗Equal Contribution

Flexible Machine Learning Methods. A priori, we might expect a nonlinear relationship between air pollution (as a covariate) and birth weight (as the response). A natural idea is to fit a flexible model — such as a (deep) Gaussian process [52, 15], transformer [70, 33], or XGBoost [10]. These methods can achieve high predictive accuracy, but the methods on their own often lack interpretability and do not immediately yield conclusions about covariate-response associations [57, 17]. Researchers have proposed a number of post hoc interpretability methods, such as Shapley values [61, 38], LIME [53], partial dependence plots [20] and Accumulated Local Effects plots [3]. These methods work directly with the fitted model and can be used to describe associations between individual covariates and model predictions. And because they are interpreting the fitted model (rather than the response itself), there is no need (or mechanism) to quantify uncertainties due to insufficient data. When the fitted model closely approximates the true response, one can view the output of these interpretability methods as describing the relationship between the covariates and response.

In many applications (e.g., in engineering, advertising, and marketing), the available data often contains enough information to be confident that the highly flexible machine learning model ap- proximates the response everywhere of interest. Conversely, in many applications in the sciences and social sciences (such as those cited above), there is often insufficient data to confidently fit a nonparametric or high-dimensional model closely to the true (latent) response everywhere relevant. In particular, in many spatial problems, there is often not enough information for a flexible method to reconstruct the response well in many spatial locations of interest. Nonetheless, we might think there could still be sufficient data to capture associations — and, by quantifying uncertainty, we can check our confidence in any conclusions. The discrepancy between applications that are data-rich and data-poor in this sense can help explain the phenomenon observed by Rudin [57]: that post hoc approximations of black-box models may be worse than fitting interpretable models directly. For example, as illustrated in a real-life recidivism case, a linear model used to approximate a black-box model can seriously misrepresent key relationships — including that between race and recidivism.

Ordinary Least Squares. So it seems natural to choose an appropriate interpretable model (rather than post hoc method) and quantify its uncertainty. Buja et al. [7] demonstrated that (directly-fit, not post hoc) linear models can be used to interpretably summarize associations even in the face of potentially nonlinear relationships (i.e., under misspecification). This observation helps explain why all of the applied studies cited above use linear regression.2 Moreover, ordinary least squares (OLS) comes equipped with classical confidence intervals. However, when the linear model is misspecified, Buja et al. [7] notes that the OLS estimator depends on the covariate values, and it follows that classical confidence intervals are valid only at the observed (training) values. Since we’re interested in valid intervals at other locations, which will have different covariate values, we need another approach. The sandwich estimator [30, 77, 78, 7] offers valid intervals under misspecification, but only if all covariates are drawn from a single distribution, which isn’t the case in our setting where we want to draw inferences at different locations from where we observe data. Thus, even though OLS provides interpretable estimates, it does not solve our key challenge: constructing valid confidence intervals in the common scenario where both (i) the linear model is misspecified and (ii) inference is needed at spatial locations that may differ from those in the observed data.

Spatial Regression Methods. One might hope that a method specifically tailored for spatial regression could address these issues. Generalized least squares (GLS) regression [2] is designed to handle spatial correlation in residuals [13, pp. 22–24], but it does not address the bias introduced by misspecification and nonrandom locations. Bayesian spatial models, such as those based on Gaussian processes, are also common, but their credible intervals tend to underestimate uncertainty in the presence of both misspecification and nonrandom locations [74, 42].

Debiasing Approaches. Another natural idea is to construct a debiased estimator of the association, and then account for the variance of this debiasing procedure. Importance weighting methods from the covariate shift literature [62] pursue this goal by reweighting each observation according to an estimated density ratio between target and source covariate distributions, aiming to remove bias from distribution shift. Semiparametric inference in partially linear models (e.g., [55, 54, 11]) takes a related approach: it first fits a flexible model to capture variation explained by a “nuisance” parameter (in our case, the spatial location) and then constructs a debiased estimator of the target association by regressing out the variation explained by this nuisance, so that the remaining signal reflects the

2In fact, Castro Torres and Akbaritabar [9, Figure 1] surveyed quantitative methods in papers up to 2022 and found that over half of those in fields such as agricultural science, social sciences, and health sciences reported results from a linear model.

direct relationship between the covariate and the response. However, these methods face two key limitations. First, they assume that the debiasing step can perfectly remove bias, which need not be true (especially in finite samples). Second, their validity relies on the assumption that observations are drawn independently and identically. And this assumption rarely holds in spatial applications, where locations are fixed and often spread unevenly due to physical, logistical, or policy constraints. We discuss these and related approaches in more detail in Appendix A.

Our Contribution. In what follows, we show through real and simulated experiments (Section 4) that existing approaches can yield confidence intervals with coverage far below the nominal level. Our principal contribution is to introduce the first method for constructing confidence intervals in spatial associations that guarantees frequentist coverage at the nominal level even when the underlying model is misspecified and inference is required at fixed, nonrandom locations that differ from those observed. We are able to account for misspecification and nonrandom locations simultaneously by making more spatially appropriate assumptions than prior work. In particular, prior work relies on a (spatially inappropriate) assumption of independent and identically distribution data; we instead assume the response is a smooth function of space observed with homoskedastic Gaussian noise. When the variance of this noise is known, our confidence intervals are valid in finite samples. To address the common case where the variance is unknown, we provide an asymptotically consistent estimator for it. In our experiments, our method is the only one that consistently attains nominal coverage (or even comes close).

Headline:  
MIT Researchers Expose Severe Failures of Traditional Confidence Intervals in Spatial Analysis, Unveil Robust Alternative for Policy-Critical Fields

News Article:  
MIT researchers have uncovered that traditional confidence intervals, widely used in spatial data analysis, can fail dramatically—sometimes by substantial margins—when applied to spatially varying variables such as air pollution [Real-World Anchor Event, MIT News]. This discovery highlights a fundamental breakdown in the statistical tools underlying much of contemporary environmental science, epidemiology, and economics, raising pointed questions about the reliability of key policy inferences that depend on robust uncertainty quantification.

The crux of the issue is that existing methods for constructing confidence or credible intervals—including those built atop flexible machine learning, classical linear regression, spatial regression, and debiasing approaches—routinely fall short of delivering nominal coverage in common spatial settings. These failures are especially pronounced in scenarios involving model misspecification and nonrandom, fixed data locations—prevalent realities in environmental and public health studies where researchers frequently need to infer associations at spatial sites unrepresented in the original data collection [Abstract; Introduction].

Traditional tools, the MIT team asserts, were never architected for the unique challenges present in spatially correlated data. For example, flexible machine learning techniques—such as deep Gaussian processes or XGBoost—may achieve high predictive fidelity but cannot, by design, deliver valid or interpretable confidence intervals relating covariates like air pollution to health outcomes. Post hoc interpretability tools (e.g., Shapley values, LIME), while informative about the behavior of fitted predictive models, do not furnish measures of statistical uncertainty anchored in the sampling process. In research domains where data is fundamentally limited or unevenly distributed across spatial locations, this distinction proves decisively consequential—uncertainties are poorly calibrated, and purported associations may be spurious [Introduction].

Similarly, classical inferential frameworks like ordinary least squares (OLS) and generalized least squares (GLS) provide confidence intervals that are valid only under stringent assumptions—specifically, model correctness or covariate overlap between observed and target locations. When applied to spatial data where models are invariably misspecified and where inference is often required for locations beyond the training sample, these intervals can radically misestimate uncertainty [Introduction]. Debiasing and importance weighting techniques from the covariate shift literature are also hamstrung in spatial contexts. Their efficacy is contingent upon the possibility of entirely removing statistical bias—a requirement that is unattainable in finite samples, particularly when the spatial allocation of observations is dictated by physical, logistical, or policy constraints, rather than random sampling [Introduction].

Addressing these deep-rooted limitations, the recent MIT-led research advances a new frequentist methodology for constructing confidence intervals for spatial associations that retains validity under model misspecification and at nonrandom, fixed spatial locations [Abstract]. The key to this method’s robustness lies in a minimal set of spatially plausible assumptions: specifically, it presumes spatial smoothness in the response and a homoskedastic Gaussian error structure, eschewing the need for model correctness or covariate overlap between observed and target locations.

Unlike its predecessors, this approach is the first to guarantee valid frequentist coverage in spatial association inference, a property that holds even in finite samples when the error variance is known. Recognizing the practical reality that noise variance is often unknown, the researchers additionally provide an asymptotically consistent estimator for this parameter, preserving coverage guarantees in large samples [Abstract]. Real and simulated experiments corroborate that existing inferential techniques frequently yield coverage far below their nominal level, while the proposed method consistently attains or closely approximates nominal coverage [Introduction].

For policy-relevant domains such as epidemiology and environmental science—where spatial associations drive decisions about air quality regulations, public health interventions, or economic resource allocation—these findings are particularly consequential. The capacity to report reliable confidence intervals not only undergirds scientific reproducibility but also ensures that policy prescriptions rest upon robust, defensible inferences, even in the face of complex data limitations.

In sum, this MIT-led work demonstrates both the perils of uncritically applying conventional statistical tools to spatially structured scientific questions, and the feasibility of rigorous alternative inference. For researchers and policymakers seeking to advance spatial data analysis beyond prediction to trustworthy estimation and uncertainty quantification, direct engagement with this methodology is now essential.

Access the full technical details and experimental validations in the primary research paper: https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2502.06067.

title: AI agents debate their way to improved mathematical reasoning
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved impressive results in complex reasoning and knowledge tasks, yet they often struggle with factual accuracy and logical consistency. Prior works have improved LLM performance using prompt-based techniques (e.g. chain-of-thought prompting and self-consistency) and post-hoc self-refinement, but these typically operate on a single model instance. Recently, multi-agent debate frameworks have emerged as a complementary approach, wherein multiple LLM agents propose answers and critique each other’s reasoning to reach consensus. Such a “society of minds” approach has been shown to significantly improve mathematical reasoning and reduce factual hallucinations. However, existing debate methods use homogeneous agents with simple majority voting, limiting their effectiveness. In this work, we propose Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD), a novel framework that extends multi-agent debate with (i) diverse specialized agents, (ii) dynamic debate routing, and (iii) a learned consensus mechanism. Each agent in A-HMAD is assigned a distinct role or expertise (e.g. logical reasoning, factual verification, strategic planning), enabling more comprehensive error-checking and perspective diversity than identical agents. A coordination policy dynamically selects which agents contribute at each round based on the question’s domain and the evolving debate state. To aggregate viewpoints, we introduce a consensus optimizer that learns to weight each agent’s vote according to its reliability and the confidence of its arguments. On six challenging benchmarks – including arithmetic QA, grade-school math (GSM8K), multifact question answering (MMLU), factual biography generation, and chess strategy – our A-HMAD consistently outperforms prior single-model methods and the original multi-agent debate baseline. Notably, A-HMAD achieves 4–6% absolute accuracy gains over standard debate on these tasks, and reduces factual errors by over 30% in biography facts. We provide extensive ablations demonstrating the benefits of agent heterogeneity, additional debate rounds, and the learned consensus module. Our findings suggest that an adaptive, role-diverse debating ensemble can drive significant advances in LLM-based educational reasoning, paving the way for safer, more interpretable, and pedagogically reliable AI systems.
introduction: Large-scale pre-trained language models have revolutionized NLP, demonstrating remarkable prowess in tasks requiring knowledge and reasoning (Brown et al. 2020; Srivastava et al. 2023). Models like GPT-3 and its successors exhibit strong few-shot learning abilities (Brown et al. 2020) and can generate coherent multi-step reasoning chains when prompted appropriately (Kojima et al. 2022). Despite these gains, even state-of-the-art LLMs often produce hallucinations – confidently stated false facts – and illogical reasoning leaps, undermining their reliability (Lin et al. 2021; Ji et al. 2023). This is due in part to the noisy, unverified nature of web-scale training data and the lack of explicit reasoning verification in standard next-token prediction training (Ouyang et al. 2022; Ziegler et al. 2019). Improving the factual accuracy and reasoning soundness of LLM outputs has therefore become a pressing research goal (Lee et al. 2022; Kadavath et al. 2022). A rich body of recent work has explored techniques to mitigate LLM reasoning and factual errors. Prompting strategies such as providing step-by-step exemplars or eliciting a chain-of-thought (CoT) have led to notable improvements in arithmetic and commonsense reasoning (Kojima et al. 2022; Wei et al. 2022). For example, Wei et al. (2022) showed that CoT prompting enables models to break down problems into logical steps, and Kojima et al. (2022) found that even zero-shot prompts (e.g. “Let’s think step by step”) can induce multi-step reasoning in LLMs. Building on CoT, self-consistency decoding samples multiple reasoning paths and selects the most common answer, which boosts correctness by averaging out reasoning variance (Wang et al. 2022). Other works equip LLMs with scratchpads or external tools: Nye et al. (2021) introduced “scratchpad” memory for intermediate computations, and tool-use approaches allow models to consult calculators or knowledge bases to check facts (Yao et al. 2023; Schick et al. 2023). Orthogonal to prompting, learning-from-feedback methods like reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) fine-tune models to favor truthful, helpful outputs (Christiano et al. 2017; Stiennon et al. 2020), significantly reducing toxic or untrue generations (Siddiky et al. 2025). Likewise, post-hoc verifier models have been trained to check and correct a model’s answers (e.g. for math word problems Cobbe et al., 2021). Another promising direction is self-reflection and refinement, where the model iteratively critiques and improves its own output (Kawamura et al. 2003; Madaan et al. 2023). Madaan et al. (2023) showed that prompting an LLM to reflect on potential errors and then attempt a revised solution can yield more accurate results, echoing human problem-solving strategies (Renze and Guven 2024). Despite these advances, most techniques above involve a single model reasoning in isolation; the model may still overlook certain errors or fail to verify all facts due to its inherent biases or blind spots (Kadavath et al. 2022; Wei et al. 2022). In contrast to single-model approaches, multi-agent frameworks draw inspiration from social or argumentative processes in which multiple experts collaborate or debate to reach a better answer (Michael et al. 2023). Early conceptual work by Minsky posited that intelligent behavior could emerge from a “Society of Mind” – a diverse collection of simpler agents. In the context of AI alignment and truthfulness, Khan et al. (2024) proposed AI debate, where two agents argue for opposing answers and a judge (human or AI) decides which argument is more convincing. The hope is that through adversarial debate, the truth would prevail because any false argument can be revealed by the opponent (Khan et al. 2024). While this work was theoretical, recent empirical studies have started to realize the benefits of multi-agent interactions. For example, Li et al. (2023) introduced a communicative multi-agent system (Camel) where agents with different roles (user, assistant, critic) collaborate to solve tasks. Similarly, MetaGPT by Hong et al. (2023) uses multiple specialized agent personas (e.g. PM, engineer, tester) to decompose and solve complex programming tasks, showing the power of role specialization. Focusing on language tasks, Kadavath et al. (2022) demonstrated that allowing two LLMs to debate from different viewpoints encourages divergent thinking and can improve answer quality. Parrish et al. (2022) found that when a less truthful LLM “debates” a more truthful LLM, the resulting answers tend to be more factual, suggesting that persuasion by a knowledgeable agent can correct the other’s misconceptions. These works hint that multi-agent interaction might overcome individual model limitations. However, some studies report mixed results; Chan et al. (2023) showed that a simple two-turn debate did not significantly help humans answer reading comprehension questions, implying that how debate is structured is critical. Most relevant to our work, Du et al. (2023) recently proposed a multi-agent debate framework for LLMs to improve both reasoning and factuality. In their approach, multiple instances of the same LLM are each prompted to generate an answer with reasoning. The agents then exchange their answers and critique each other’s reasoning over several rounds, before outputting a final consensus answer. This method, requiring only black-box access to the models’ outputs, was shown to significantly enhance performance on math word problems, strategic games, and factual QA, compared to single-agent baselines. Notably, Du et al. reported that debate reduces hallucinations: agents often identify and remove one another’s uncertain or inconsistent facts, leading to a final answer with more reliable information. The multi-agent debate can be seen as inducing an ensemble-like effect where multiple reasoning paths are explored in parallel and vetted against each other, yielding higher confidence when they converge. Zhang et al. (2023) further applied a debating agents idea for LLM-based evaluation, showing that a multi-agent debate can refine judgment of answers. Wu et al. (2024) used debates between a “student” and “professor” model to supervise and improve unreliable reasoning, while Zelikman et al. (2022) leveraged cooperative agents for embodied decision-making. These underline a growing consensus that multi-agent interactions can enhance an LLM’s performance and reliability. Despite the promise of multi-agent debate, there remain clear opportunities for improvement. First, prior debate frameworks largely use homogeneous agents – e.g. identical GPT-3.5 models with the same prompting style. This limits the diversity of perspectives; if all agents share similar strengths and weaknesses, certain errors may go unchallenged. Recent works in multi-agent collaboration emphasize heterogeneity as a key factor for success (Li et al. 2023; Hong et al. 2023), which suggests that introducing specialized agents could further improve the debate outcome. Second, existing methods rely on a simple majority vote or unanimous agreement for the final answer. This treats all agents as equally reliable, ignoring the possibility that some agents (or certain argument lines) might be more likely correct than others. A more nuanced consensus mechanism that weights agents by their past accuracy or the confidence of their justifications could lead to more robust final answers. Third, the debate protocol in prior work is static – agents alternate in a fixed order and number of rounds. In complex scenarios, a rigid protocol might be suboptimal: for example, if a particular agent has expertise most relevant to the query, the framework should allocate more opportunity for that agent to contribute, while bypassing irrelevant agents to save computation. Such dynamic agent routing or adaptive scheduling of debate rounds has not been explored in existing work. Finally, no prior study (to our knowledge) has integrated an explicit learning component into the debate loop – the interplay is handled by prompting alone. This raises the question of whether parts of the multi-agent debate process (e.g. how to aggregate answers) can be learned from data to further boost performance. In this paper, we propose a new framework called Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD) that addresses the above limitations and substantially improves LLM factuality and reasoning via multi-agent debate. Our contributions are summarized as follows: (1) We design a heterogeneous agent ensemble where each agent has a specialized role or distinct model type, along with a dynamic routing strategy that activates different subsets of agents depending on the query type and intermediate debate outcomes. (2) We propose a learned consensus module that replaces simple majority voting for final answer selection, weighting agents based on features of their responses and confidence. (3) We thoroughly evaluate A-HMAD on six challenging benchmarks, achieving new state-of-the-art results for our base model, and significantly reducing hallucinations and incorrect facts compared to single-agent and prior debate baselines. (4) Through controlled ablations and qualitative case studies, we show why our components matter and how expert agents can correct others’ errors. Together, these advances move us closer to robust, truth-seeking AI systems that can reliably solve complex problems and self-correct errors.

Headline:  
Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate: Advancing Multimodal Reasoning for Amazon’s Global Catalog

News Article:

At the heart of Amazon’s worldwide customer experience lies the Amazon Selection and Catalog Systems (ASCS), a platform responsible for orchestrating the identification and relationship mapping of billions of products across petabytes of multimodal data. The technical challenge is both clear and profound: accurate product identity and relationship inference at an unprecedented scale is not only fundamental to ASCS’s operational success, but represents a foundational AI problem in large-scale trustworthy multimodal reasoning. Existing approaches to catalog understanding must contend with ambiguous item descriptions, incomplete and noisy data, and the necessity of robust, auditable decisions across languages, images, and structured metadata. As recent research and developments—such as Amazon’s deployment of multi-agent frameworks for catalog auditing and LLM-based judge systems—underscore, scalable, reliable reasoning is a sine qua non for both the scientific and commercial advancement of such platforms [Anchor: Granting Early-Stage Reasoning Freedom in Multi-Agent Debate, Amazon Science].

Within this landscape, the emergence of Adaptive Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Debate (A-HMAD) represents a technically significant breakthrough. Large Language Models (LLMs), even those demonstrating impressive few-shot learning and chain-of-thought prompting capabilities, have struggled with factual consistency and logical rigor—limitations rooted in both the noise of web-scale pretraining data and the absence of explicit verification steps in next-token prediction [Abstract; Introduction]. Although advances such as chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting, self-consistency decoding, and post-hoc reflective refinement offer improvements within single-model frameworks, they leave critical gaps: models may still propagate hallucinations or overlook logical inconsistencies, particularly in the context of complex, uncertain tasks such as catalog reasoning.

A-HMAD directly addresses these challenges by introducing a multi-agent debate framework defined by three principal innovations: heterogeneity of agent expertise, dynamic debate routing, and a learned consensus optimizer. Each agent in the A-HMAD architecture is independently specialized—for example, one may focus on logical inference, another on factual verification, a third on strategic planning—thereby ensuring coverage of a broader error surface than can be achieved by homogeneous “copies” of a single LLM [Abstract; Introduction]. In operational terms for ASCS, this diversity is directly applicable: logical experts can rigorously resolve category hierarchies, factual experts can cross-verify multimodal evidence for item identities, and strategic planners can optimize routing of ambiguous catalog entries for further adjudication.

The dynamic routing policy embedded in A-HMAD selectively engages agents based on both the domain of the catalog query and the intermediate state of the ongoing debate. For ASCS, this representational adaptability enables the system to allocate computational and human-in-the-loop resources where they are most impactful—for instance, invoking visual verification agents when faced with image-text mismatches, or emphasizing factual specialists during entity deduplication. Such routing is not a fixed pipeline but is learned and adapted as debate unfolds, directly paralleling the operational necessity for agility in handling billions of dynamically changing catalog items.

Traditional debate frameworks in LLM research often resort to simple majority voting for final decision aggregation, treating all agent outputs as equally reliable. In the context of Amazon’s need for high-stakes catalog decisions—where errors can propagate at scale—this uniform treatment is suboptimal. A-HMAD’s learned consensus optimizer departs from this paradigm by differentially weighting agent votes according to agent reliability and the confidence levels embedded in their arguments [Abstract]. This granular learning mechanism is essential for uncertainty calibration, a fundamental ASCS requirement: it enables fine-grained trust estimation and interpretability around high-ambiguity or high-value choices (e.g., the grouping of similar but distinct products, the resolution of conflicting seller or supplier data, etc.).

Benchmark evaluations detailed in the A-HMAD research underpin the operational significance of these design choices. On tasks ranging from arithmetic and grade-school math to multifact question answering, factual biography generation, and even chess strategy, the A-HMAD ensemble consistently outperforms both single-agent and prior homogeneous multi-agent debate baselines—yielding up to 4–6% absolute accuracy gains and reducing factual error rates by over 30% relative to standard debate methods [Abstract]. The technical fidelity of these results is central to their relevance for ASCS: in a petabyte-scale catalog, incremental improvements in accuracy and error reduction directly translate into quantifiable reductions in customer-facing errors, catalog drift, and trust breaches.

The architectural and empirical advances of A-HMAD signal a critical trajectory for the future of agentic reasoning in industrial-scale AI deployments like ASCS. By systematically integrating heterogeneous expert agents, adaptive routing tailored to data domain and task, and a reliability-weighted consensus mechanism, A-HMAD offers both a blueprint and an immediate technical substrate for advancing multimodal reasoning, uncertainty management, and autonomous identity inference in e-commerce catalog systems. Its direct applicability to ASCS’s operational imperatives—augmented by the growing need for automated, interpretable product decisions—positions this framework at the nexus of research and deployment.

For practitioners and researchers dedicated to the integrity of large-scale AI reasoning in high-stakes contexts, the full A-HMAD study is essential reading. Access the complete research at: https://dx.doi.org/10.1007/s44443-025-00353-3.

anchor_event.full_content:
At Amazon Selection and Catalog Systems (ASCS), our mission is to power the online buying experience for customers worldwide so they can find, discover, and buy any product they want. We innovate on behalf of our customers to ensure uniqueness and consistency of product identity and to infer relationships between products in Amazon Catalog to drive the selection gateway for the search and browse experiences on the website. We're solving a fundamental AI challenge: establishing product identity and relationships at unprecedented scale. Using Generative AI, Visual Language Models (VLMs), and multimodal reasoning, we determine what makes each product unique and how products relate to one another across Amazon's catalog. The scale is staggering: billions of products, petabytes of multimodal data, millions of sellers, dozens of languages, and infinite product diversity—from electronics to groceries to digital content. The research challenges are immense. GenAI and VLMs hold transformative promise for catalog understanding, but we operate where traditional methods fail: ambiguous problem spaces, incomplete and noisy data, inherent uncertainty, reasoning across both images and textual data, and explaining decisions at scale. Establishing product identities and groupings requires sophisticated models that reason across text, images, and structured data—while maintaining accuracy and trust for high-stakes business decisions affecting millions of customers daily. Amazon's Item and Relationship Platform group is looking for an innovative and customer-focused applied scientist to help us make the world's best product catalog even better. In this role, you will partner with technology and business leaders to build new state-of-the-art algorithms, models, and services to infer product-to-product relationships that matter to our customers. You will pioneer advanced GenAI solutions that power next-generation agentic shopping experiences, working in a collaborative environment where you can experiment with massive data from the world's largest product catalog, tackle problems at the frontier of AI research, rapidly implement and deploy your algorithmic ideas at scale, across millions of customers. Key job responsibilities Key job responsibilities include: * Formulate open research problems at the intersection of GenAI, multimodal reasoning, and large-scale information retrieval—defining the scientific questions that transform ambiguous, real-world catalog challenges into publishable, high-impact research * Push the boundaries of VLMs, foundation models, and agentic architectures by designing novel approaches to product identity, relationship inference, and catalog understanding—where the problem complexity (billions of products, multimodal signals, inherent ambiguity) demands methods that don't yet exist * Advance the science of efficient model deployment—developing distillation, compression, and LLM/VLM serving optimization strategies that preserve frontier-level multimodal reasoning in compact, production-grade architectures while dramatically reducing latency, cost, and infrastructure footprint at billion-product scale * Make frontier models reliable—advancing uncertainty calibration, confidence estimation, and interpretability methods so that frontier-scale GenAI systems can be trusted for autonomous catalog decisions impacting millions of customers daily * Own the full research lifecycle from problem formulation through production deployment—designing rigorous experiments over petabytes of multimodal data, iterating on ideas rapidly, and seeing your research directly improve the shopping experience for hundreds of millions of customers * Shape the team's research vision by defining technical roadmaps that balance foundational scientific inquiry with measurable product impact * Mentor scientists and engineers on advanced ML techniques, experimental design, and scientific rigor—building deep organizational capability in GenAI and multimodal AI * Represent the team in the broader science community—publishing findings, delivering tech talks, and staying at the forefront of GenAI, VLM, and agentic system research

Title: Unfixing the mental set: Granting early-stage reasoning freedom in multi-agent debate

The Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) team is seeking a dedicated, skilled, and innovative Applied Scientist with a robust background in machine learning, statistics, quality assurance, auditing methodologies, and automated evaluation systems to ensure the highest standards of data quality, to build industry-leading technology with Large Language Models (LLMs) and multimodal systems. Key job responsibilities As part of the AGI team, an Applied Scientist will collaborate closely with core scientist team developing Amazon Nova models. They will lead the development of comprehensive quality strategies and auditing frameworks that safeguard the integrity of data collection workflows. This includes designing auditing strategies with detailed SOPs, quality metrics, and sampling methodologies that help Nova improve performances on benchmarks. The Applied Scientist will perform expert-level manual audits, conduct meta-audits to evaluate auditor performance, and provide targeted coaching to uplift overall quality capabilities. A critical aspect of this role involves developing and maintaining LLM-as-a-Judge systems, including designing judge architectures, creating evaluation rubrics, and building machine learning models for automated quality assessment. The Applied Scientist will also set up the configuration of data collection workflows and communicate quality feedback to stakeholders. An Applied Scientist will also have a direct impact on enhancing customer experiences through high-quality training and evaluation data that powers state-of-the-art LLM products and services. A day in the life An Applied Scientist with the AGI team will support quality solution design, conduct root cause analysis on data quality issues, research new auditing methodologies, and find innovative ways of optimizing data quality while setting examples for the team on quality assurance best practices and standards. Besides theoretical analysis and quality framework development, an Applied Scientist will also work closely with talented engineers, domain experts, and vendor teams to put quality strategies and automated judging systems into practice.

title: Decoding black box AI with humanreadable data descriptions and influence
abstract:
Jaeyeon Bae* Ulsan National Institute of Science and Technology(UNIST) {chaerikim, qowodussla, taehwankim}@unist.ac.kr

Deep learning models have been successful in many areas but understanding their behaviors still remains a black-box. Most prior explain- able AI (XAI) approaches have focused on in- terpreting and explaining how models make predictions. In contrast, we would like to un- derstand how data can be explained with deep learning model training and propose a novel approach to understand the data via one of the most common media - language - so that hu- mans can easily understand. Our approach pro- poses a pipeline to generate textual descrip- tions that can explain the data with large lan- guage models by incorporating external knowl- edge bases. However, generated data descrip- tions may still include irrelevant information, so we introduce to exploit influence estimation to choose the most informative textual descrip- tions, along with the CLIP score. Furthermore, based on the phenomenon of cross-modal trans- ferability, we propose a novel benchmark task named cross-modal transfer classification to examine the effectiveness of our textual descrip- tions. In the experiment of zero-shot setting, we show that our textual descriptions are more effective than other baseline descriptions, and furthermore, we successfully boost the perfor- mance of the model trained only on images across all nine image classification datasets. These results are further supported by evalu- ation using GPT-4o. Through our approach, we may gain insights into the inherent inter- pretability of the decision-making process of the model.
introduction:
Introduction

Deep learning models have successfully been ap- plied to various fields and achieved high perfor- mance (Huang et al., 2017; Vaswani et al., 2017; He et al., 2016; Dosovitskiy et al., 2020). Despite the rapid performance improvement, understand- ing their behaviors remains a black-box. While most prior explainable AI (XAI) approaches have focused on interpreting and explaining how models make predictions, there are few attempts to explain the data. We would like to understand data since it is one of the most important elements of deep learning performance. Among deep learning models, we try to describe image classes with humaninterpretable language. For example, in the case of frog class: smooth, moist skin with coloration ranging from green to brown, often featuring various patterns and markings. Recent studies (Menon and Vondrick, 2023; Maniparambil et al., 2023; Pratt et al., 2023) have utilized large language models (LLMs), pre-trained on vast datasets, to generate textual descriptions that aid vision model performance. However, identifying descriptions that are truly informative and semantically aligned with the class remains a key challenge. To solve this problem, we propose novel approach to identify the most helpful textsfor model training among those generated by the LLM, using influence estimation and CLIP scores. Influence estimation (Koh and Liang, 2017; Pruthi et al., 2020) calculates the positive or negative impact of each training image in predicting a test sample, while CLIP scores measure the similarity between an image and text. Unlike most previous research that applies influence scores only to images, we extend this method to texts by integrating CLIP scores to identify helpful texts for model training. We name the helpful text as proponent text and the score for determining the proponent text as Influence scores For Texts (IFT).

To obtain the proponent texts, we first write down all the features of each image class with GPT- 3.5 (Brown et al., 2020). Instead of merely query- ing the LLM for class features, we employ a two- stage prompting process using Wikipedia urls to integrate external knowledge bases. This approach not only compensates for potential gaps in detailed knowledge of LLM but also helps mitigate halluci-

These authors contributed equally to this work.

Figure 1: Overview of our framework. (1) ’Generating Textual Descriptions of Each Dataset’ process generates the textual descriptions that can explain each class well from given Wikipedia urls, pre-defined prompts, and class names (2) ’Choosing Proponent Texts using IFT’ process determines the proponent texts using IFT. (3) ’Cross-Modal Transfer Classification’ process first trains model with images, then further trains it with proponent texts.

nations. Among the generated textual descriptions, IFT filters out unhelpful information and retains only proponent texts. Furthermore, leveraging the concept of cross-modal transferability (Zhang et al., 2023), which allows text to serve as input for mod- els originally trained with images, we apply this principle to our approach. We retrain the image- trained model using proponent texts, assigning weights based on the IFT. We call this ’cross-modal transfer classification’. Compared to image-only training and baselines that generate textual descrip- tions, our approach achieves superior performance on most of the nine image datasets, demonstrating that our textual descriptions are more helpful in model training than other baselines. Additionally, we evaluate the helpfulness, informativeness, and relevance of the generated descriptions using GPT- 4o (Hurst et al., 2024) and find that our method consistently outperforms all baselines across these criteria. Figure 1 shows the overview of our frame- work.

In sum, our contributions are as follows:

We propose a novel approach to identify help- ful textual descriptions that effectively explain each image class, achieving superior perfor- mance in the zero-shot setting compared to baseline descriptions.

By using the proposed IFT defined as the sum of the influence score and the CLIP score, we can pinpoint the most helpful textual descrip- tions for model training. Proponent texts se- lected through IFT contain only relevant in- formation that aids in accurate image classifi- cation. Furthermore, we may get an inherent interpretability of where the black-box model focuses on during training.

We propose cross-modal transfer classifica- tion as a novel benchmark task based on the cross-modal transferability phenomenon. Ex- tensive experiments with nine datasets show

that the proponent texts are informative and describe each image class well.

Headline:  
From Black Box to Clarity: Leveraging Textual Descriptions and Influence Estimation to Illuminate Deep Learning Decisions

News Article:  
Deep learning models remain widely regarded as “black boxes” due to the opacity of their decision-making processes, a persistent barrier for both transparency and trustworthiness in AI-driven systems. The urgent demand for explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) has intensified as these models are deployed in high-stakes applications. A critical real-world anchor, as discussed in recent technology analysis, highlights an emerging pathway: translating training data into human-readable descriptions using large language models (LLMs) and multimodal evaluation. This direction promises to enhance model transparency by clarifying the otherwise inscrutable data and internal mechanics that shape AI learning outcomes [Real-World Anchor Event].

In direct response to this challenge, researchers from UNIST have introduced a novel approach that reorients the focus of XAI from solely explaining model predictions to explaining the very data underpinning model training (Bae et al., 2025) [Abstract, Introduction, Citation]. This technical innovation centers on a carefully designed pipeline that generates and rigorously filters textual descriptions for image classes, with the goal of rendering the data itself more interpretable and actionable for domain experts.

At the core of this pipeline is a multi-stage process that marries the generative capabilities of large language models with structured external knowledge—specifically, Wikipedia URLs—to construct comprehensive, human-interpretable textual descriptions for each image class. Rather than relying on unconstrained LLM output, the pipeline employs a two-stage prompting mechanism that integrates knowledge bases, thereby not only expanding the scope of semantic coverage but also mitigating potential hallucinations endemic to LLM generation [Introduction].

However, the challenge of aligning generated textual descriptions with both model requirements and the statistical nuance of visual datasets necessitated a new filtering paradigm. The proposed method advances prior work by introducing a dual-criterion scoring mechanism: Influence scores For Texts (IFT). The IFT metric is synthesized by summing the influence estimation—a quantification of the impact a training data sample exerts on model predictions, inspired by methodologies such as those by Koh and Liang (2017)—with the CLIP score, which quantitatively measures the semantic similarity between a text and its associated image class [Abstract, Introduction]. This extension of influence estimation from raw images to textual descriptions enables the system to algorithmically isolate the most “proponent” texts: those that substantively guide model training toward more accurate decision boundaries.

The bridge between this technical advance and broader AI transparency goals is explicit. As with the real-world push toward systems that render their rationale in accessible language, the pipeline ensures that only descriptions with high IFT are prioritized—those that both exert a meaningful impact on model predictions and are semantically coherent with the visual data. As described in the anchor event, this approach directly addresses the need for model transparency by not simply auditing outputs, but clarify the informational substrate of learning itself, with high-impact human-readable descriptions anchoring trust in AI deployments.

The researchers introduce a new benchmark task, cross-modal transfer classification, as a principled test of these filtered textual descriptions [Abstract, Introduction]. Leveraging the cross-modal transferability phenomenon, the methodology enables a model initially trained on images to be further trained with proponent texts, each weighted according to its IFT. This paradigm operationalizes the alignment between visual and textual data modalities, evaluating the capacity of such descriptions to improve model accuracy and stability in a zero-shot setting across nine diverse image classification datasets.

Empirical results underline the potency of the approach: models retrained with IFT-selected proponent texts outperform those using either image-only supervision or baseline textual descriptions (including those generated by LLMs without filtering). These findings are corroborated using evaluation from GPT-4o, which confirms superior helpfulness, informativeness, and relevance relative to prior baselines [Abstract, Introduction]. Such results not only buttress the technical merit of the methodology, but also point toward enhanced interpretability of the learning process—making transparent which semantic regions of the data exert critical influence during model training.

By systematically bridging multimodal alignment, influence estimation, and language-guided data representation, this research marks a methodological advancement in the quest for explainable AI. The explicit identification and utilization of high-impact textual descriptions, jointly scored via IFT, operationalize a new axis of interpretability—shifting explanatory focus from the model’s outputs to the data that shapes its internal logic.

For those invested in advancing rigorous, trustworthy AI, this work offers both a conceptual and practical foundation for transparent model training. The full paper is strongly recommended for researchers seeking to further explore the intersection of LLMs, multimodal alignment, and interpretability. Access the original work at [https://dx.doi.org/10.18653/v1/2025.emnlp-main.1717].

anchor_event.full_content:
Deep learning models are often described as "black boxes" due to the opacity of their decision-making processes. A new approach focuses on translating training data into human-readable descriptions, enhancing transparency by clarifying the data that underpins AI learning. Using large language models and external knowledge sources, this method generates and evaluates data descriptions based on their influence on model predictions and alignment with visual content. Experiments show that models trained with high-impact descriptions achieve greater stability and accuracy, marking a significant advancement in explainable AI and fostering more transparent, trustworthy systems.

The amnesia of modern AI: Every time we teach an AI something new, we risk breaking something old. In deep learning, this is called Catastrophic Forgetting—when a model absorbs new information and unintentionally overwrites prior knowledge. It’s why most production models are effectively frozen in time. To teach them new facts, we often have to retrain the entire system from scratch. That may be changing. A new paper from Google Research (NeurIPS 2025) introduces a promising approach called Nested Learning. For example, think about how GPS handles a road closure: - Traditional AI: It’s like redrawing the entire city map from scratch because of one blocked street. It's a massive and expensive effort for a minor change. - Nested Learning: It’s like a live traffic update. The system displays the detour in real-time, while the underlying map remains accurate and unchanged. This is how it works: Instead of storing everything in one "flat" block of memory, the model is structured in layers: - A Stable Core: This holds long-term knowledge—reasoning, language, and fundamental principles. This layer is protected and stays stable to maintain reliability. - An Adaptive Layer: This handles short-term information—recent news, user preferences, or current events. This layer can be updated or replaced without touching the foundation. New information is treated as active context, not carved into the permanent foundation. This makes "Evergreen AI" possible—systems that stay current with new data without losing their foundational performance. Check out the paper below!

Here are 30 definitions that help you understand AI. (📌 save this post for later) 1. Artificial Intelligence (AI): A computer brain that can think and solve problems. 2. Machine Learning (ML): AI that gets smarter by looking at lots of real examples. 3. Deep Learning (DL): AI that learns in small layers, just like building blocks. 4. Neural Networks (NN): A computer brain parts that work to solve big puzzles. 5. Model Architecture: A giant map that shows how an AI brain is built. 6. Training Data: Huge piles of info used to teach an AI new lessons. 7. Pretraining: When an AI reads many books before it starts to talk. 8. Fine-Tuning: Teaching an AI a special skill after it learns basics. 9. Reinforcement Learning (RL): AI learns by trying to earn points for doing a task. 10. Overfitting: When AI memorizes one thing but cannot learn others. 11. Context Window: How much an AI remembers about what you said before. 12. Tokenization: Breaking big words into tiny pieces for AI to study. 13. Embeddings: Changing words into numbers so the AI can understand. 14. Transformer Model: A special way for AI to see how words fit together in order. 15. Large Language Model (LLM): A very smart AI that is built to chat with you using text or audio. 16. Generative AI (GenAI): AI that creates new text, images and video and very fun stories. 17. Text-To-Image: Making a brand new picture from only your own words. 18. Chatbot: A robot friend you can talk to when you need answers. 19. Computer Vision (CV): Helping a robot see and understand an image or video. 20. Agents: AI that can choose a job and then go do it for you. 21. Autonomous: A smart machine that can run mostly all by itself. 22. Algorithm: A list of rules that an AI must follow to do a job. 23. Bias: When an AI is unfair because its lessons were wrong. 24. Zero-Shot Learning (ZSL): AI doing a new task it was never actually taught yet. 25. Hallucination: When an AI makes up a story that is not really true. 26. Privacy: Keeping your private info very safe from the internet. 27. Ethics: Making sure we use AI in a way that is good and fair. 28. Prompt Engineering: Finding the best way to tell an AI what you want it. 29. Multimodal: AI that can see and hear at the exact same time too. 30. Natural Language Processing (NLP): Helping a computer talk and listen just like people. AI doesn’t have to be confusing!
